string.text:
%c[0,153,255,255]AN-94 “Abakan”%c[0,140,140,140] - the 5.45 mm Nikonov AN-94 (“Abakan”), a model of individual combat automatic small arms chambered for the 5.45 mm intermediate cartridge (5.45x39), and a component of the automatic rifle weapons system.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The AN-94 rifle was developed during the experimental design program “Abakan” at the design bureau of the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant under the leadership of G. N. Nikonov, and was adopted into service in 1997 under the designation “5.45 mm Nikonov assault rifle, model 1994 (AN-94).” Production of the AN-94 rifles was carried out by the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant.
 
The AN-94 is an individual weapon intended to engage enemy manpower and unarmored samples of weapons, military, and special equipment. All types of 5.45 mm automatic rifle cartridges (5.45x39) are used for firing.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: assault rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (RF)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1998–2008

%c[0,153,255,255]ASh-12%c[0,140,140,140] - Russian large-caliber assault rifle, developed by TsKIB SOO for the needs of special forces of the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) together with the VSSK sniper rifle.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
It is a “weapon-patron” complex, as a new large-caliber cartridge 12,7x55 mm PS 12 with several types of bullets: armor-piercing with protruding core, jacketed, double-bullet with tandem bullet placement, etc. was developed for it in TsKIB SOO.
 
Due to the high stopping power of a heavy bullet of large caliber, the ASh-12 assault rifle is a highly effective close combat weapon.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2010-present

%c[0,153,255,255]AK-103%c[0,140,140,140] - export version of the AK-74M assault rifle, differing by the use of 5.56x45 mm (NATO standard SS109) and 7.62x39 mm (Soviet 1943 model cartridge) cartridges, respectively.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 7.62 mm Kalashnikov assault rifle AK-103 was developed based on the standard assault rifle of the Russian army AK-74M to replace the classic Kalashnikov assault rifle for the 7.62x39 mm cartridge.
 
AK-103 is built on the "classic AK scheme," distinguished by its reliable operation, including in difficult operating conditions, high manufacturability, and ease of use by personnel with any level of training.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1994-present

%c[0,153,255,255]AK-12 model 2012%c[0,140,140,140] - Russian advanced assault rifle chambered for the 5.45x39 cartridge, adopted by the Russian army in January 2018.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 The first prototype was created by the Kalashnikov Concern in 2011 and presented to the public in January 2012. The AK represented a fundamentally new assault rifle, implemented using the general scheme and technical solutions of previous Kalashnikov rifles. The development was carried out by the leading design team of the Kalashnikov Concern under the direction of Vladimir Zlobin on their own initiative since 2012. The AK-12 is considered a fifth-generation Kalashnikov assault rifle (in order: AK; AKM; AK-74; AK-74M; AK-12).
 
The most noticeable improvement from the user's point of view was the enhanced ergonomics - complete symmetry of the rifle, a convenient safety-selector, and the introduction of a bolt catch. This allowed the rifle to be fully operated with only one hand, suitable for both right-handed and left-handed users. During the refinements, the receiver cover attachment and other details were changed, but the distinctive features of the rifle make it easily distinguishable from traditional AKs.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: RF
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2011-present

%c[0,153,255,255]AKS-74N%c[0,140,140,140] - "Night" variant of the folding AKS-74, with a side rail for mounting night sights.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 The 5.45-mm Kalashnikov assault rifle model 1974 (GRAU index - 6P20) is a 5.45 mm caliber assault rifle, developed in 1970 by Soviet designer M. T. Kalashnikov and adopted by the armed forces of the USSR in 1974. The AK-74 assault rifle was created to replace the 7.62-mm Kalashnikov assault rifle AKM and its modifications in the design bureau of the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant under the leadership of M. T. Kalashnikov and adopted in 1974 under the designation "5.45-mm Kalashnikov assault rifle model 1974 (AK-74)." Simultaneously with the AK-74 assault rifle, its modifications with a folding stock AKS-74 and with a night sight AK-74N and AKS-74N were developed.
 
The development of the AK-74 is associated with the transition to a new low-impulse 5.45x39 mm cartridge. It was first used in the Afghan war, and later in all conflicts in the post-Soviet space. Currently, this assault rifle is used by most countries in Eastern Europe, as well as Asia.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1974-present

%c[0,153,255,255]AKS-74UN%c[0,140,140,140] - 5.45x39 Kalashnikov folding short assault rifle, AKS-74U, (GRAU Index - 6P26) - shortened version of the AKS-74.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 It was developed in the late 1970s - early 1980s for arming crews of combat vehicles, aircraft, artillery crews, as well as paratroopers. After the end of World War II and the widespread adoption of the AK family of small arms in the Armed Forces of the Soviet Union, there were no longer any mass-produced submachine guns (SMGs) in service. After the creation of the AK-74, the idea arose to create a weapon for paratroopers/tank crews, APCs, etc.
 
In 1980, under the brand name AKS-74U (6P26), the assault rifle began to be supplied to the USSR troops. It is also used by the Ministry of Internal Affairs and security structures due to its compact size. In terms of all mechanisms, the AKS-74U is completely analogous to the AK-74, except for the hammer turn limiter installed instead of the rate reducer.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1979-1993

%c[0,153,255,255]AK-105%c[0,140,140,140] - compact Kalashnikov assault rifle of the "AK-100 Series" Developed for the 5.45x39 mm cartridge for the Russian army and law enforcement agencies. This model has a 45-round magazine, a more comfortable grip, and a fore-end with a Picatinny rail for mounting sights.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 The external difference from the AK-74M assault rifle is a shortened barrel and the appearance of a flash suppressor. It replaces the outdated Soviet AKS-74U in the army. The AK-105 is equipped with a side rail for mounting optical or collimator sights, as well as a suppressor. The presence of a folding stock ensures ease of handling the weapon when moving on the march, transporting, and parachuting, while also allowing shooting with the stock folded.
 
Modern materials are used in the design. The stock, magazine, fore-end, receiver cover, and pistol grip are made of plastic and are highly impact-resistant and resistant to external influences. The use of protective coatings ensures high corrosion resistance of metal parts. Used by special forces of the CIS.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1994-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Steyr AUG A1%c[0,140,140,140] (Armee Universal Gewehr - Army Universal Rifle) - The small arms complex, released in 1977 by the Austrian company Steyr-Daimler-Puch (now Steyr Arms GmbH).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 In Australia, the rifle is produced under license under the brand name F88. It has interchangeable barrels of different lengths: the main 508 mm, as well as shortened barrels of 350 mm and 407 mm and a heavy barrel of 621 mm. This rifle uses a bullpup configuration, in which the magazine and bolt assembly are located behind the fire control handle and trigger.
 
There are two firing modes: single shots and automatic fire. It is also possible to upgrade for single shots and three-round bursts. The AUG was original in many respects, with one of the main advantages of the rifle being its modularity.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Austria
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1978-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Steyr AUG A3%c[0,140,140,140] (Armee Universal Gewehr - Army Universal Rifle) - The small arms complex, released in 1977 by the Austrian company Steyr-Daimler-Puch (now Steyr Arms GmbH).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 The Steyr AUG A3 differs from the base model by the presence of four Picatinny rails: the top one is used for mounting sights, an underbarrel grenade launcher or vertical foregrip can be attached to the bottom rail, and laser aiming devices or other necessary additional equipment can be attached to the side rails (this particular model only has the top rail).
 
Additionally, it features a bolt release button and is equipped with a 42-round magazine.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Austria
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1978-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Accuracy International AWM%c[0,140,140,140] - a British sniper rifle developed by Accuracy International for the Magnum rifle cartridge. It comes equipped with an optical scope and is part of the AW (Arctic Warfare) series.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140]
 In 1996, the British company "Accuracy International" developed and began production of the sniper rifle "Accuracy International AWM". Essentially, it is a rifle from the "Arctic Warfare" series modified for a more powerful Magnum cartridge, which allows for increased shooting range and accuracy. Structurally, it differs from the Accuracy International L96A1 / Arctic Warfare only in the firing and feeding mechanisms (bolt, magazine), which were redesigned to handle the larger dimensions and higher pressures of Magnum cartridges.
 
In 2009, this rifle set a record for precision shooting distance. British sniper Craig Harrison hit two enemies in Afghanistan at a distance of 2477 meters using the L115A3 rifle. In 2012, Accuracy International ceased production of the Arctic Warfare Magnum (AWM) rifles; production of the Arctic Warfare (AW) rifles was also soon discontinued. They have been replaced by more advanced rifles from the Accuracy International AX / AXMC series.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: United Kingdom
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1996-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Browning B725%c[0,140,140,140] - Model B725 from Browning Arms Company is a further development of the popular B25 vertical shotgun. The manufacturer has improved the design and created a very ergonomic shotgun with excellent performance.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The B725 shotgun can be used in various fields, from hunting and sports to law enforcement and self-defense. Production of the hunting smoothbore shotgun B725 is carried out by the Browning foreign (BELGIUM) enterprise. It looks solid, majestic, and gives the impression of powerful weaponry that can be relied upon even in harsh conditions of the CEZ.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Belgium
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2012-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Beretta M9A1%c[0,140,140,140] - a self-loading pistol chambered in 9x19mm Parabellum, used by all branches of the armed forces of the United States. It is based on the Italian Beretta 92F pistol.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Compared to the Italian-made 92F pistols, it features a simplified and shorter production cycle, with some parts manufactured through automated processes instead of handmade by Italian gunsmiths. One of the main advantages of the Beretta M9 is its reliability and durability. However, the pistol has its drawbacks. It is quite heavy and cumbersome for carry, especially during prolonged use.
 
M9A1 - a 2006 modification featuring a Picatinny rail under the barrel (for attaching tactical lights, laser aiming devices, and other accessories). Overall, the Beretta M9 pistol remains one of the most recognizable and popular handguns in the world, known for its reliability, accuracy, and ease of use.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Italy/USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1990-present

%c[0,153,255,255]PP-19 Bizon%c[0,140,140,140] - a submachine gun model 19, developed in 1993 by V. M. Kalashnikov (son of the designer M. T. Kalashnikov) and Alexey Dragunov (son of E. F. Dragunov) commissioned by the Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Russian Federation (MVD Russia).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The weapon was based on the AKS-74U design (achieving 60% parts commonality), but its operation is based on the energy of the free bolt recoil. A distinctive feature of this submachine gun is its helical underbarrel magazine with a capacity of up to 64 rounds. The muzzle end of the barrel is equipped with a compact flash suppressor with two wide rectangular ports. The barrel cover is plastic. A standard mounting rail for sights is located on the left side of the barrel housing.
 
This submachine gun has been adopted by the Russian law enforcement agencies. Serial production began in 1996, and the model was produced in limited numbers. The "Bizon" is chambered for the Makarov 9x18mm cartridge. The 9x18mm cartridge was a standard for pistols and submachine guns in the Soviet Union and Warsaw Pact countries. However, it is less powerful and has lower armor-piercing capabilities compared to the standard 9x19mm Parabellum cartridge.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1996

%c[0,153,255,255]TOZ-66%c[0,140,140,140] - well-known to experienced hunters. According to users, the Tula Arms Plant managed to embody all the advantages of previous hammer-fired shotgun models in this double-barreled gun.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The creation of the TOZ-66 12-gauge hunting shotgun was a true gift to Soviet hunters who had long dreamed of obtaining a classic Tula hammer-fired shotgun in this caliber. The Tula Arms Plant produced various categories of TOZ-66 hammer-fired shotguns. The most common were standard grade shotguns with birch stocks. In addition, improved models with beech stocks were also available for sale. Custom TOZ-66 shotguns were distinguished by walnut stocks and better fitting of components.
 
The TOZ-66 is the strongest of the Tula hammer-fired double-barreled shotguns. It is suitable for both professionals and novice hunters. Overall, the TOZ-66 12-gauge shotgun is reliable, simple, and elegant hunting weaponry. Notably, even modern TOZ double-barreled shotgun models significantly lag behind the strength of the TOZ-66, which has a reinforced barrel housing %c[0,153,255,255](to switch to double-shot mode, use the fire mode switch key)%c[0,140,140,140].
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1968-1974

%c[0,153,255,255]Colt M1911A1%c[0,140,140,140] - a self-loading pistol chambered in .45 ACP. Designed by John Moses Browning in 1908 (U.S. Patent 984519, February 14, 1911). The M1911A1 is a model introduced in 1926.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
It was created over 100 years ago, has seen two world wars and numerous smaller conflicts, and is still in service today. It is produced by numerous companies worldwide and is actively used by military, police, competitive shooters, and civilians. Norway was the first foreign country to adopt the M1911. Both American-made and local, licensed or unlicensed versions of the M1911 have spread around the world. Russia also did not remain indifferent, ordering a batch of "Colts" during the First World War.
 
On March 29, 1911, the Colt pistol was officially adopted by the U.S. Army as the "Model 1911". The pistol was issued to officers and non-commissioned officers of all branches of the U.S. armed forces from 1911 to 1985 and is still authorized for use by military and law enforcement personnel as personal defense weapons.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1911-present

%c[0,153,255,255]M45A1%c[0,140,140,140] - produced by Colt under the index M1070CQBP is the most modern version of the famous M1911 family from the legendary American firearms manufacturer, adapted for current operations.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The enhanced M45A1 includes several changes from the original M1911A1 design. One of its features is the dual recoil spring system, which distributes the recoil force of the .45 ACP cartridge by reducing the peak impulse recoil force. It also features Tritium night sights with 3 dots, a 5-inch barrel, ambidextrous safety, Picatinny rail, and a Desert Tan Cerakote finish.
 
The current M45A1 does not have the "USMC" marking on the slide for either military or civilian sales. The Marine Corps Headquarters directed Colt to discontinue the marking on pistols. All M45A1 pistols currently bear the marking "COLT M45A1".
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1911-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Desert Eagle%c[0,140,140,140] - a large-caliber semi-automatic pistol (up to 12.7 mm). It is marketed as hunting, sporting, and self-defense weapon against wild animals and criminal threats.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Developed in 1983 in the USA by Magnum Research, with subsequent modifications by Israel Military Industries, which later took over production of these pistols. Due to its size and intimidating appearance, the Desert Eagle is often used in movies, television shows, and video games. Over the years, the Desert Eagle has been released in several main variants: Mark I and Mark VII, Mark XIX, as well as several less popular versions like the smaller Baby Eagle and Micro Desert Eagle. The pistols differ primarily in caliber, and some also in size.
 
The ability to shoot monstrous 12.7 mm cartridges from a semi-automatic pistol is what the Desert Eagle was originally created for. By the way, before the creation of the "Eagle," it was generally believed in the design community that creating a well-functioning semi-automatic pistol for such a cartridge was likely impossible.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA/Israel
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1983-present

%c[0,153,255,255]DVL-10 Sniper Rifle%c[0,140,140,140] - a family of modern Russian sniper rifles by Vladislav Yevgenievich Lobaev.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The DVL-10 is a lightweight, compact, and silent rifle designed for special combat tasks requiring special accuracy and range, using subsonic and existing ammunition. It is equipped with a folding stock, which is unified with other SVL models. The rifle comes with a match-grade stainless steel barrel by LOBAEV Hummer Barrels. It does not have a single fixed model. The caliber and other characteristics of this rifle vary depending on customer preferences. The barrel is made of stainless steel, and the rifle is equipped with a Picatinny rail for mounting various types of sights. These rifles are known for their extremely high accuracy.
 
In addition to the above, it can be added that, unlike standard issue weapons, all Lobaev rifles are modular weapons with the ability to be precisely adjusted to the specific task facing the shooter. However, the "Saboteur" is one of the most expensive representatives of the category of silent weapons.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2010-present

%c[0,153,255,255]F-1%c[0,140,140,140] (slang - "lemon") - a hand anti-personnel defensive grenade. The grenade is designed to engage live forces in defensive combat. Due to the significant fragmentation radius (up to 50 meters), it can only be thrown from cover, an armored personnel carrier, or a tank.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The F-1 grenade belongs to the hand anti-personnel fragmentation defensive grenades of remote action. Its design proved to be so successful that it has remained virtually unchanged to the present day. It has been slightly modified and refined to improve the reliability of the fuse mechanism. In open terrain, the effective engagement range against the enemy during the grenade explosion by the high-explosive action of the projectile is 3-5 meters. The radius of continuous fragmentation damage to live forces is 7 meters. The chances of being hit by grenade fragments are maintained at distances of up to 200 meters. Additionally, F-1 grenades are often used for setting ambushes.
 
As for the nickname "lemon", it originated during the First World War. Many believe that the grenade was named after its characteristic shape, but this is not true. The truth is that the F-1 grenade, which we commonly refer to as "lemon" today, is nothing more than a copy of the French hand grenade of the 1915 model of the same brand, known in French as F.1.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: hand grenade
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1940-present

%c[0,153,255,255]FN Five-seveN%c[0,140,140,140] - a semi-automatic pistol developed and manufactured by the Belgian company Fabrique Nationale d'Herstal. The name is a combination of "Five-seveN," where "5.7" refers to the caliber of the firearm, which is .224, and "FN" represents the initials of Fabrique Nationale.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
As a companion pistol to the FN P90 submachine gun, the Five-seveN shares many characteristics. The 5.7x28mm cartridge for the P90 submachine gun was developed by FN in the early 1990s, and the Five-seveN was developed by FN in the late 1990s as a pistol using the same cartridge. Since its introduction, it has also been offered in various commercial versions, most recently the Five-seveN USG. The 5.7x28mm cartridge for the Five-seveN was initially developed alongside the P90 and introduced in the 1990s. This cartridge was later improved and introduced in 1993 as the 5.7x28mm SS190. Following the release of the SS190 cartridge, FN officially announced the Five-seveN pistol in 1995. A prototype of the pistol was presented to the public in 1996. With some modifications, the double-action-only variant of the pistol went into production in 1998.
 
The FN Five-seveN has gained increasing popularity on the civilian market, and its characteristics have also made it popular in special operations and similar fields. By 2009, the Five-seveN was used by over 300 law enforcement agencies in the United States.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Belgium
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1998-present

%c[0,153,255,255]FN F2000%c[0,140,140,140] - a Belgian bullpup assault rifle developed by FN Herstal. The FN F2000 was first introduced in 2001. This weapon is designed for combat tasks in modern local military conflicts. It was developed in the late 1990s and has been in serial production since 2001-2002.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The F2000 operates on a gas-operated action, with the locking mechanism achieved by rotating the bolt. It features a bullpup layout. Thanks to the patented front ejection of spent casings (casings are ejected outwards near the muzzle), the main problem of such configuration - the inability to use the weapon by left-handed shooters due to ejected casings hitting the face - is solved. Despite this, the weapon can be used by both left-handed and right-handed shooters. The body is made of polymers, and the stock is a lightweight, detachable plastic that can be replaced with various additional modules: laser sights, flashlights, and a 40mm grenade launcher. The standard sight is a 1.6X magnification optical sight with a wide field of view. It can be quickly replaced with any other sight that has corresponding mounts, such as a specialized computerized fire control module.
 
The F2000 Tactical is similar to the standard model, but lacks an optical sight. Instead, it has an additional Picatinny rail and open iron sights.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Belgium
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2001-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Fort-12%c[0,140,140,140] - Ukrainian semi-automatic pistol chambered in 9x18mm PM.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The pistol was developed in the early 1990s by the NPO "Fort" at the request of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Ukraine due to the moral and physical obsolescence of the Soviet Makarov pistols in service. The development utilized the experience of the Czech firearms company Ceska Zbrojovka. The first experimental batch of Fort-12 pistols was manufactured in May 1995. Trials and experimental use of the Fort-12 pistols were completed in 1997, and in June 1998, the experimental design work was completed. Serial production (using equipment from the Czech Republic) began in 1998. Barrels were supplied from the Czech Republic until 2002.
 
The Fort-12 differs from the PM with improved ergonomics, increased shooting accuracy, reduced felt recoil, higher rate of fire, and larger size and weight. The only exception is the ejector, which, according to some shooters, needed to be shortened to prevent deformation and bolt jamming. The Fort-12 pistol can be equipped with a tactical flashlight LT-6A, a laser sight, and (in the Fort-12B variant) - a suppressor.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Ukraine
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1998-present

%c[0,153,255,255]OTS-33 Pernach%c[0,140,140,140] - Russian automatic pistol, developed in the mid-1990s by a team of designers from TsKIB SOO under the leadership of I.Ya. Stechkin to replace the APS pistol.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The OTs-33 is based on the design of the OTs-23 "Drotik" automatic pistol. The Pernach is an automatic pistol designed to replace the Stechkin APS in various special units of the OMON of the Russian police, the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Russia (MVD), and other militarized formations. The weapon is almost entirely made of steel, with plastic grips. The pistol is fed from standard double-row magazines with a capacity of 18 rounds or from extended magazines with a capacity of 27 rounds. Under the barrel, there are rails on the frame of the weapon for attaching a combat flashlight or laser sight.
 
Currently, due to the incorporation of TsKIB SOO into the Tulsky KBP, the production and promotion of the OTs-33 are handled by KBP. Small quantities of the OTs-33 are in service with various units of the MVD of the Russian Federation. Compared to the Stechkin APS pistol, the OTs-33 has a significantly simpler design and a lower rate of fire, making it more convenient to handle.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia

%c[0,153,255,255]HK G28%c[0,140,140,140] - Sniper Rifle developed and manufactured by the German company Heckler & Koch on order of the Bundeswehr (German army).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
This rifle emerged in response to the needs of German troops operating in Afghanistan as a weapon to support small infantry units. The sniper rifle Heckler-Koch HK G28 became a conceptual counterpart to the Soviet Russian Dragunov SVD rifle. The sniper rifle Heckler-Koch HK G28 is based on the sporting and hunting self-loading rifle HK MR308, which, in turn, is a civilian version of the automatic rifle HK 417. It is fed with detachable box magazines with capacities of 10 or 20 rounds.
 
The HK G28 rifle can currently be used in two configurations, interchangeable at the military base - "standard" and "patrol". The standard configuration of the HK G28 rifle includes an elongated barrel, a telescopic adjustable stock with a cheek rest, a folding bipod, as well as a Schmidt Bender PMII 3-20/50 optical sight with an attached laser rangefinder. The patrol configuration allows the rifle to be lightened for use in foot patrols, with a shortened and lighter barrel, a lightened adjustable stock without a cheek rest, and a Schmidt Bender PMII 1-8/24 sight. Additionally, various types of night sights and laser pointers can be mounted on the rifle.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2014-present

%c[0,153,255,255]HK G36%c[0,140,140,140] - a family of firearms developed in the early 1990s by the German company Heckler & Koch, designated internally as HK 50, to replace the well-known automatic rifle HK G3.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
In 1995, the G36 assault rifle was adopted by the Bundeswehr, and in 1999, it was adopted by the armed forces of Spain. Additionally, the G36 is used by the police in the United Kingdom and is supplied to several other countries for law enforcement and military use. The G36 is fed from transparent plastic magazines of 30 rounds, which have special mounts for combining magazines into "packs" to facilitate quick reloading. Since the magazine receiver of the G36 is made according to NATO standards, the HK G36 rifle can use any standard magazines, including the Beta-C double drum magazines with a capacity of 100 rounds.
 
From a design standpoint, the mechanics of the weapon are based on the AR-18 rifle, developed in the early 1960s in the USA, but made with extensive use of modern materials, including high-strength polymers for the receiver. In March 2015, the German federal government and the Ministry of Defense acknowledged that the G36 had significant accuracy problems and its use should be restricted due to overheating during prolonged firing or in high ambient temperatures. In April 2015, the German Ministry of Defense announced the decision to gradually phase out all 167,000 G36 rifles from service.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1997-present

%c[0,153,255,255]HK MG36%c[0,140,140,140] - a light machine gun based on the G36. It has a heavy barrel near the magazine well and bipod. Several hundred units were produced in 1995, but serial production was canceled the same year due to dissatisfaction among Bundeswehr soldiers with the duration of continuous fire.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Like the assault rifle, the machine gun features a carrying handle with an integrated three-times magnification optical sight, a folding stock, and a muzzle compensator/flash suppressor. It is fed from a 30-round magazine. A special variant of the Beta C-magazine for the G36 was developed to increase the ammunition capacity, holding 100 rounds and designed for prone firing. The MG36 has a cyclic rate of fire of 750 rounds per minute. Despite having a thicker barrel, the MG36's capabilities for fire support are quite limited.
 
The MG36 was intended for German military service. A total of 4,700 units were ordered in 1995, but the program was subsequently canceled in 1997. The MG36 was produced in limited numbers. It was not officially adopted by any country. An export version of the machine gun, designated MG 36E, was produced with a less powerful 1.5-times magnification optical sight.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]Galil ACE%c[0,140,140,140] - an Israeli family of automatic weapons, further development of the Galil rifle. The family includes the ACE 2x (caliber 5.56x45 mm) and 3x (caliber 7.62x39 mm) assault rifles, as well as the 5x automatic rifles (caliber 7.62x51 mm), which are further divided into variants distinguished by barrel length. The Galil ACE 22 is designed for the 5.56x45 mm cartridge with a barrel length of 332 mm.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Galil ACE family of rifles (assault rifles, automatic rifles) was developed and produced by the Israeli weapon company IWI Ltd. The design of the Galil ACE family is based on the Galil rifle, which in turn is a development of the AK series of rifles. The Galil ACE rifles focus on ergonomics, ease of use, compatibility with additional accessories, and versatility in application. The main differences from its predecessors include relocating the charging handle to the left side of the weapon (allowing for easier operation with the left hand without disengaging the right hand from the fire control handle), the addition of a bolt catch, integrated Picatinny rail guides for optics and accessories, and an adjustable telescopic stock.
 
Currently, the 5.56 mm Galil ACE rifles are in service with the Colombian Army and are produced under license by the Colombian manufacturer Indumil. Additionally, Israeli-produced Galil ACE rifles are available for export.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]Gauss Rifle (also known as "Item No. 62")%c[0,140,140,140] - a sniper rifle that uses an electromagnetic system to accelerate bullets, powered by the energy of the artifact "Flash".
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
It is a rifle with a metal body, a black carbon fiber stock, and an integrated scope with magnification adjustment. Around the barrel of the weapon, there are 6 magnetic coils that accelerate the projectile. It is an extremely accurate weapon created by designers in the Zone. It operates almost silently because it uses artifact energy rather than gunpowder gases for firing. Despite the tremendous power of the shot, the recoil and rate of fire of the Gauss Rifle are very low (the capacitors require several seconds to recharge for a new shot). In terms of accuracy and stopping power, it surpasses any classical firearms. However, finding this rifle in the expanses of the Zone is extremely difficult.
 
There is also unconfirmed information about the existence of an "M" version of the rifle in the territory of the Chernobyl Exclusion Zone (CEZ). The nature of this version and its approximate location are unknown.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Ukraine

%c[0,153,255,255]Glock 17%c[0,140,140,140] - an Austrian pistol developed by Glock for the Austrian Armed Forces.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Glock 17 was the first firearm developed by this company. The resulting model proved to be quite successful and convenient to use, which led to its adoption by the Austrian Armed Forces under the designation P80. Due to its combat qualities and reliability, it gained wide popularity as a civilian self-defense weapon. The pistol's design feature includes the absence of a safety lever and hammer. The pistol is mostly made of high-strength, heat-resistant plastic, making the Glock 17 lightweight and extremely durable. The operating principle is "draw and shoot," with the safety hidden in the trigger, preventing accidental discharge without fully pressing the "safe action" trigger.
 
Thanks to its successful design, the pistol has gained wide popularity worldwide. Various modifications of the Glock 17 are used by the armies and police forces of over 30 countries, including Austria, Sweden, and Norway. The model is NATO-approved as one of the main models of personal firearms. Glock pistols of various modifications are used by the police and special forces of the USA, Canada, Mexico, India, the Philippines, Saudi Arabia, and many other countries.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Austria
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1982-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Glock 18%c[0,140,140,140] - an Austrian automatic pistol produced by Glock GmbH chambered in 9x19mm Parabellum. Designed for special units of the military and police.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Glock 18 was developed in 1986 for the EKO Cobra (Einsatzkommando Cobra) counter-terrorism unit of the Austrian Federal Police, which needed a lightweight compact weapon capable of firing in bursts. The Glock 18 is based on the Glock 17 pistol and is distinguished primarily by its selector switch that allows for single-shot or burst fire. In burst mode, it has a rate of fire of approximately 1200 rounds per minute. Variants are available that can fire bursts of three rounds or fully automatic fire, but not in the same model. The Glock 18 also differs in the size of the frame and slide rails, trigger group components, and barrel, designed to prevent interchangeability with other models, thus preventing modification of semi-automatic pistols allowed in the civilian market into automatic weapons.
 
The Glock 18 attracted interest from police and military forces, although it did not gain the expected popularity due to the limited effectiveness of automatic fire compared to even compact submachine guns. Overall, the Glock 18 inherits all the strengths of the base Glock 17 pistol and is very similar in construction.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Austria
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1989-present

%c[0,153,255,255]OTS-14 "Groza"%c[0,140,140,140]- Russian assault rifle-grenade launcher complex developed by TsKIB SOO in 1993. It represents a variant of the OTS-12 "Tiss" assault rifle (in turn created based on the AKS74U), made in a bull-pup scheme. The rifle uses 9x39 mm intermediate cartridges (SP-5, SP-6, and PAB-9), which were used in the VSS, AS "VAL", and SR-3.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
It is equipped with a simplified version of the detachable underbarrel grenade launcher GP-25 "Kostyor" (the simplification primarily consists of the absence of a pistol grip with a trigger and trigger hook). This configuration allowed for the creation of a compact powerful weapon. Automatic and grenade launcher firing are controlled by a single trigger hook. Switching between "automatic-grenade launcher" mode is carried out by a button switch located on the right side of the barrel box. An open-type sight is mounted on a P-shaped stand, which can serve for carrying the weapon in hand. It is possible to install a suppressor, laser sight, optical sight, and tactical flashlight. The complex is 70% unified with the AKS74U.
 
The modular design allowed the use of the rifle with an extended muzzle and front grip, or with a replacement suppressor, as well as a rifle-grenade launcher complex. "Groza" underwent military trials during the First Chechen Campaign in 1995-1996. OTS-14-4A - assault weapon (rifle with grenade launcher).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]•%c[0,140,140,140]Type: rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]•%c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]•%c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1993-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Browning Hi-Power%c[0,140,140,140] - self-loading pistol designed by John Browning and Dieudonne Saive with a high-capacity magazine. Development was completed in 1935, and production continues to this day under various names in many countries.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The pistol was initially designed for the French military but was not adopted by France. Various variants were used by many countries during World War II and beyond. The pistol's name is somewhat misleading, as it actually reflects the high capacity of its 13-round magazine, significantly higher than its closest competitors in 1935 - Luger P08 (8 rounds), Colt 1911 (7 rounds), Mauser C96 (10 rounds). The pistol is often simply referred to as "Hi Power", even in Belgium. Other common names include HP (from "Hi-Power" or "High-Power") or GP (from French "Grande Puissance"). The claimed effective range for models with adjustable sights using a shoulder stock is 500 m, and for the basic model, it is 50 m.
 
It is known for its simplicity and reliability, which have contributed to its long production and worldwide use. Belgian company FN Herstal ceased production of the military variant and civilian models in 2017, but the pistol continues to be produced under license in Argentina. Canadian manufacturer John Inglis Co., Ontario, produced the Hi-Power for its country's armed forces, Britain, other Commonwealth countries, and for export for a long time. The main visual feature of the Mark III is the absence of a water drain hole under the muzzle at the front of the slide.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Belgium

%c[0,153,255,255]Knife Bars A-2607%c[0,140,140,140] - forged knife made of 95X18 steel with a handle made of precious wood and a brass guard.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Bars knives are well known among knife enthusiasts in the CIS. Production is located in the city of Vorsma, in the Nizhny Novgorod region. The company adheres to classic forms and proven materials in knife production. Model A-2607 is one of the most popular models for this company. It is produced in different versions, with different steels and handle materials. The most affordable option, of course, is the 95X18 steel with a Walnut handle. These knives are simple and inexpensive, which does not make them bad.

%c[0,153,255,255]M9 Bayonet%c[0,140,140,140] - military bayonet knife. The blade of the M9 bayonet is single-edged with a sawback.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The M9 bayonet was developed by Charles A. "Mickey" Finn, owner of the company "Qual-A-Tec," as an improved version of the bayonet for the AKM 6X3. The M9 was designed to replace the obsolete M7 bayonet. The primary motivation behind the design of this knife was the desire to create a bayonet that would function more as a tool than a weapon. It is a versatile tool that allows not only cutting but also chopping, stabbing, opening ammo crates, cutting barbed wire (including under tension), and performing various other tasks. The M9 bayonet was adopted by the US Army in 1986 and is still in production.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA

%c[0,153,255,255]OTS-04 Knife%c[0,140,140,140] was developed by the Tula Central Design Bureau (TSKIB) in the late 1980s to early 1990s and was intended for special units of the Ministry of Internal Affairs (MVD).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The abbreviation "OTS" stands for "Oruzhie TSKIB" (Weapons TSKIB). The knife is characterized by a modern aggressive design. The spear-shaped blade is single-edged. The blade has long descents. There is a false edge on the front part of the spine, and the majority of the spine is a saw with small teeth. The knife has a very robust construction, with a spine thickness of 7 mm. The blade has a slight bevel at the front. There is a double-row saw on the spine of the blade, but due to the small height of the teeth, its effectiveness is relatively low, especially when sawing green wood. The blade allows for powerful thrusting strikes, with chopping and cutting strikes being less effective.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)

%c[0,153,255,255]MTech USA Xtreme Knife%c[0,140,140,140] is a fully black, brutal, all-metal tactical knife from the company Mtech.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The blade of the Mtech Tactical Fighter knife is made of 440 chrome-bearing steel, known for its high corrosion resistance, wear resistance, strength, and hardness. It has a tanto and a large serration on the spine. The blade has a black anti-glare finish which improves wear resistance, enhances the strength of the cutting edge, and reduces adhesion tendency. There are notches near the handle where it is convenient to place the thumb during forceful cutting. The handle has pronounced finger grooves made of fiberglass-reinforced nylon, a durable and impact-resistant material that handles moisture well and has increased resistance to fire.

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: present

%c[0,153,255,255]KM2000 Knife%c[0,140,140,140] - German combat knife of the Bundeswehr.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The knife is manufactured by the German company Eickhorn-Solingen Ltd. It is adopted by the German Bundeswehr. The blade is in the "American tanto" style. The blade is single-edged, with a serrated edge on the lower part of the blade (the part closest to the guard). The sheath is made of carbon-plastic with a metal tip. The handle of the KM2000 is 40% fiberglass with polyamide resin. The spine is straight with a wedge-shaped profile. The tang, passing through the entire handle, protrudes from its back, allowing it to be used as a hammer. The front side of the sheath has an area of abrasive material with diamond coating for blade sharpening.

%c[0,153,255,255]SA-80%c[0,140,140,140] (SA-80 - Small Arms 1980) - a British small arms complex developed in the mid-1980s consisting of the L85 rifle, L86 light machine gun, L22 carbine, and L98 cadet rifle. The complex is in service with the British Army.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Since 1974, development was carried out for a specially designed 4.85x49mm cartridge, but after NATO adoption of the 5.56x45mm (SS109) cartridge, both models were redesigned for it. As a result, the 5.56mm SA-80 system was created, including the L85A1 rifle and L86A1 light machine gun, with 80% of their parts being interchangeable. The recoil is much lower than that of larger caliber models, which simplifies training and reduces muzzle climb during firing. The bolt group mechanism, locking scheme, and gas system were borrowed from the American AR-18 rifle. Due to a number of shortcomings, including low rifle reliability, in 2000 the German firm Heckler & Koch received a contract to modernize it. As a result, the British Army received the L85A2, which was significantly improved, and complaints (according to developers' statements) about its "capriciousness" and unreliability ceased.
 
SA-80 is configured in a "bullpup" layout - the magazine is located behind the trigger, and it can be equipped with a 4x optical sight. This rifle has high accuracy in single shots as well as bursts of 2 shots. In early 2011, the British military contingent in Afghanistan received over 100,000 Magpul EMAG plastic magazines. In August 2018, the UK Ministry of Defence announced its intention to phase out the 5.56x45mm L86A2 LSW auxiliary rifle, designed to increase combat distance, in favor of the L129 A1 marksman rifle chambered in 7.62x51 NATO.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: United Kingdom
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1985-present

%c[0,153,255,255]M4%c[0,140,140,140] - automatic carbine, created in the USA based on the M16A2 rifle and initially intended for arming vehicle crews and artillery units. M4A1 - a variant developed in 1994, which allows for continuous burst fire similar to the M16A2E3.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
However, the US Special Operations Command adopted this carbine as standard for all US special operations forces. Currently, the entire US Army has transitioned to it, as the convenience of a shortened carbine in modern conditions, where much of the army consists of motorized infantry, vehicle crews, and support troops, more than compensates for the slight reduction in its characteristics compared to the rifle. The main differences between the M4 and the M16A2 are the shorter barrel and the retractable telescopic stock. The M4A1 variant differs from the M4 in its firing mode: SAFE/SEMI/AUTO, but is otherwise identical to the M4.
 
It is often stated that the M4 is an M16 rifle with a shortened barrel and telescopic stock. In reality, the situation is somewhat more complex: while the M16 is based on the AR-10 platform, and the M4 is indeed based on the same base model - the AR-15 rifle, their differences are not limited to barrel length and stock construction. There are other relatively minor differences.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1994-present

%c[0,153,255,255]M249 SAW%c[0,140,140,140] - a variant of the FN Minimi light machine gun for the US Army, caliber 5.56x45 mm, made in the USA (produced by FN Manufacturing, Inc., a local subsidiary of the Belgian company FN Herstal), serial production started in the 1980s.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
In the USA, after the adoption of the 5.56 mm M16 assault rifle (M16A1) of the Stoner system, numerous attempts were made to create a light machine gun for the same cartridge. Development began in the early 1960s; initially, the weapon was designed for the M193 5.56x45 mm cartridge, but later it was converted to the SS109 5.56x45 mm cartridge. Prototypes of the weapon appeared in the early 1970s, but serial production of the machine gun started only in the 1980s. The machine gun is produced in Columbia, South Carolina, by the local FN subsidiary. In the late 1970s, the US Marine Corps and Army jointly established requirements for a light machine gun for infantry units. Trials were conducted on several systems: the American "Stoner-86", CMG-2 "Colt", XM233 "Merrimon", "Rodman", XM234 "Filko-Ford", as well as the Belgian FN "Minimi". After rigorous testing, the high reliability in various conditions was demonstrated by the "Minimi", which was adopted in 1984 by the US Marine Corps and Army under the designation M249 SAW.
 
The machine gun entered service in the armed forces of several countries, including: Australia, Belgium, Canada, France, Greece, Spain, Italy, Indonesia, Malaysia, New Zealand, Thailand, Sweden, the United Kingdom, and the United States (M249 SAW). The weapon is currently manufactured at FN Herstal factories, as well as under license in Australia, Greece, and the USA. Indirect competitors of the Minimi machine gun abroad include the German HK MG4 and the Israeli "Negev".
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Light machine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Belgium/USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1984-present

%c[0,153,255,255]M320%c[0,140,140,140] - the M320 underbarrel grenade launcher was created to replace the M203 underbarrel grenade launcher.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Picatinny Arsenal conducted a competition for a new 40 mm underbarrel grenade launcher. Heckler & Koch won the competition in early 2006. The M320 was based on the Heckler & Koch AG36, but they are not completely identical. Production began in November 2008. The grenade launcher was officially unveiled in July 2009 at Fort Bragg, North Carolina. The M320 grenade launcher consists of three main parts: the launcher assembly, a day/night sight from Insight Technology Inc, and a handheld laser rangefinder.
 
Some advantages of the M320 include: it can be used in two ways. It can be mounted under the barrel of M16 and M4 rifles, or it can be used standalone. The sight on the grenade launcher is foldable. This avoids problems encountered with the M203 model. The sight on the M203 was mounted on top of the launcher assembly, which could interfere with aiming from the rifle; to solve this problem, the sight was made foldable. The M320 can fire all NATO grenades - high explosive, smoke, and illuminating.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2008-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Remington M700%c[0,140,140,140] - a magazine-fed rifle made in the USA.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
There are many variants of the rifle for different calibers. The standard cartridge is 7.62x51 mm NATO (.308 Win). Technically, it is a magazine-fed rifle with a longitudinally sliding bolt action. The magazine capacity depends on the caliber and can range from 3 to 5 rounds. It is the flagship product of Remington Arms. The rifle can be equipped with optics from various manufacturers. Developed based on the Mauser brothers' rifle. Production of the rifle began in 1962. The Model 700 has various modifications and is widely used in the USA as a large-caliber sniper, sports, and hunting rifle, produced in a wide range of calibers from .17 Rem (4.5 mm) to .338 Lapua Magnum (8.6 mm).
 
The rifle has high accuracy (from 1 MOA for hunting modifications to 0.5 MOA for target rifles) and high manufacturing quality. The popularity of the rifle is also due to the large number of aftermarket parts produced by third-party companies. Customization of this rifle model for end users' needs by private gunsmiths is widespread.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1962-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Desert Tech MDR%c[0,140,140,140] (abbreviation for micro dynamic rifle) is a family of bullpup semi-automatic rifles developed by Desert Tech (formerly Desert Tactical Arms) in 2014.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Desert Tech MDR automatic rifle was developed by the American company Desert Tech LLC (DesertTech, formerly Desert Tactical Arms). The rifle was first shown to the public in early 2014 at the ShotShow in the USA, and serial production was planned to begin in 2015. Built in a bullpup configuration, this rifle in its base variant is a modular, multi-caliber automatic weapon designed for arming special operations police and military units. Additionally, based on it, a semi-automatic civilian version is planned to be released, however, according to the company's representative, the automatic version will be the main one.
 
The MDR rifle is designed to be ambidextrous. The rifle is compatible with Desert Tech caliber conversion kits, allowing the rifle's caliber to be changed. This provides a unique feature where the rifle chassis can accept both intermediate cartridges and full-power rifle cartridges with the same serial number.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]Mosin Nagant%c[0,140,140,140] - magazine-fed rifle adopted by the Russian Imperial Army in 1891.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Production of the rifle began in 1892 at the Tula, Izhevsk, and Sestroryetsk arsenals. Due to limited production capacities at these plants, an order for 500,000 rifles was placed at a French armory in Chatellerault. In the early years after adoption, changes were made to the original design during production and use of the weapon. For instance, in 1893, a wooden handguard was introduced to protect the shooter's hands from barrel heat. In 1896, a new bolt was implemented, longer and with a larger head that did not pass through the barrel, simplifying the cleaning of the weapon. The serrations on the sides of the magazine cover were removed to prevent them from wearing out the uniform during carrying. These improvements were also made to previously manufactured rifles. On March 21, 1897, the 500,000th rifle was released. By the start of the Russo-Japanese War, approximately 3,800,000 rifles had been supplied to the army. In 1931, 154,000 rifles were produced; in 1938, 1,124,664; and in 1940, 1,375,822.
 
The rifle is also known by other names, such as the 7.62 mm Rifle Model 1891 (1891/30), Three-Line Rifle, Mosin Rifle, "Mosinka," and similar. The name "Three-Line" comes from the caliber of the rifle's barrel, which equals three lines (an obsolete unit of length equal to one-tenth of an inch or 2.54 mm). It was mass-produced from 1892 until the late 1950s (in the USSR and other countries), during which it underwent multiple modernizations. Based on the Model 1891 rifle and its modifications, a whole range of sporting and hunting weapons were created, both rifled and smoothbore.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]MP-133%c[0,140,140,140] - a Russian smoothbore shotgun used for various types of hunting, sports shooting, security, and self-defense. The shotgun is based on the IZh-81 and has been serially produced by the Izhevsk Mechanical Plant since 2000.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The reloading mechanism is a pump action. The barrel locking is done by a tilting breech block located in the bolt (wedge), which, when in the firing position, enters a notch in the receiver's breech. Unusual for pump-action shotguns, the hammer-fired trigger mechanism is in the form of a detachable unit equipped with a triple safety system: preventing firing with the bolt not fully locked; a block preventing the bolt from unintentionally unlocking before firing; and a non-automatic safety blocking the trigger. The shotgun's receiver is made of an aluminum alloy, while the hardware (fore-end, stock) is made of wood or plastic.
 
The shotgun is fed from an under-barrel tubular magazine, with a standard capacity of 4 rounds (expandable by installing magazine extenders). The barrels are equipped with interchangeable chokes. The MP-133 shotguns are produced in several different modifications and versions, featuring interchangeable muzzle attachments for shooting lead and steel shot, and various options for stocks and fore-ends.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: pump-action shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2000-2013

%c[0,153,255,255]MP-153%c[0,140,140,140] - a Russian semi-automatic smoothbore shotgun, popularly known as "Murka," used for various types of hunting, sports shooting, law enforcement, and self-defense. Production was replaced by the MP-155 shotgun starting from 2011.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The MP-153 shotgun uses a gas-operated mechanism with a ring gas piston located around the magazine tube. The gas-operated unit design includes an automatic gas regulator (located around the magazine tube) that vents excess powder gases into the atmosphere. Parts exposed to powder gases (barrel channel, chamber, piston, gas chamber, outer surface of the magazine tube) are chrome-plated. The valve and piston rings are made of stainless steel. The shotgun is equipped with an automatic bolt hold-open feature: after firing the last round, the bolt stops in the rearward position, facilitating and speeding up reloading and signaling to the shooter that the ammunition is expended.
 
The magazine is tubular and under-barrel, with a capacity of 4 rounds. The magazine capacity can be increased to 8 rounds by installing an extension. MP-153 shotguns are produced in several different configurations: with interchangeable muzzle attachments for lead and steel shot, without a sighting rib, with a front sight, without interchangeable muzzle attachments, and with various stock and fore-end options.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: semi-automatic shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2000-2011

%c[0,153,255,255]MP-43%c[0,140,140,140] - a shotgun for enthusiasts of classic hunting. Smoothbore guns with horizontally arranged barrels have been produced at the Izhevsk Mechanical Plant for over 55 years and have a reputation for being inexpensive, exceptionally reliable, and possessing excellent shooting characteristics.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The MP-43 is a descendant of the beloved Soviet hunting shotgun, the Izh-58, and its design differs very little from its predecessor. It uses the same locking principle with a lower plate latch, although simplified, similar trigger mechanisms, and so on. The horizontal MP-43 is the most common and in-demand type of hunting weapon in modern Russia. Its popularity is due to its extremely high working resource - it guarantees 15,000 shots. In addition to reliability, this shotgun has excellent shooting characteristics and allows for uniform shot distribution at distances up to 50 meters.
 
High safety in handling the shotgun during walking hunts and in dense forest areas is ensured by a reliable automatic safety mechanism that locks hammers and trigger hooks. The shotgun's good balance provides comfortable and quick aiming. To protect against corrosion, the barrels and chambers are chrome-plated. The shotgun is produced with both fixed chokes and interchangeable muzzle attachments, ensuring consistent shooting accuracy. The stock and forend of the MP-43 shotgun and its modifications are made of high-quality walnut or beech.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: Over 55 years (ongoing)

%c[0,153,255,255]HK MP5%c[0,140,140,140] - a family of submachine guns developed by the German firearms manufacturer Heckler & Koch (HK) in the 1960s based on the HK G3. Widely known as the GSG9 and similar special forces weapons, as well as a frequent guest in cinema and video games.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The MP5 submachine gun was first introduced by Heckler & Koch in 1966 under the designation HK54. This name was chosen according to the old model numbering system of the company: the number "5" indicated the weapon as a submachine gun, and the number "4" indicated that the submachine gun was designed for the 9x19mm Parabellum cartridge. It received its modern name after the government of the Federal Republic of Germany adopted it in mid-1966 for police and border guard use as the Maschinenpistole 5 or MP5 for short. Structurally, the MP5 is essentially a scaled-down version of the German HK G3 rifle adapted for pistol cartridges. It inherited from the G3 a stamped steel receiver with a downward-folding buttstock and a detachable trigger group module, as well as a semi-free bolt with delayed roller locking. The MP5 always fires from a closed bolt, which increases accuracy and shot groupings but also increases the risk of cartridge cook-off under heavy use.
 
As of 2001, HK MP5 submachine guns and their variants have been adopted and used by armies, police forces, and special units in over 50 countries worldwide. One of the most well-known users of the MP5 is the United States Navy SEALs. A special variant of the MP5, the MP5SD, was created for special operations with an integrated suppressor. The success of the MP5 is attributed not only to Heckler & Koch's successful marketing but also to its reliability, ease of handling, and high shooting accuracy.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1966-present

%c[0,153,255,255]HK UMP45%c[0,140,140,140] - a submachine gun developed by the German company Heckler & Koch in the 1990s as a complement to the HK MP5 family of submachine guns.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The design of the new weapon was simplified, but more modern materials were used. The company planned to offer this model on the US police weapons market, which influenced the choice of ammunition used - the popular .45 ACP and .40 S&W cartridges, with a variant for the 9x19mm Parabellum appearing a year later. The HK UMP submachine gun is designed with a free bolt mechanism. It fires from a closed bolt. The design primarily considered the stopping power of the chosen cartridge. Overall, the weapon turned out to be quite reliable and easy to maintain. The folding stock makes the UMP submachine gun quite convenient compared to its counterparts. Originally developed for firing large-caliber cartridges, and due to difficulties in feeding .45 ACP compared to 9x19mm Parabellum, the practical rate of fire was artificially reduced to 600 rounds per minute, making it one of the "slower" submachine guns.
 
The design of the submachine gun allows for the installation of various reflex sights, tactical lights, and devices for silent and flameless shooting. Additionally, the UMP can be equipped with a detachable grip. It can mount four Picatinny rails - on the top, right, left, and bottom of the receiver. A vertical front grip facilitates control of the weapon during automatic fire.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1999-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Brugger & Thomet MP9%c[0,140,140,140] - a compact and rapid-firing weapon produced by the Swiss firearms company Brugger & Thomet. It is used by special forces units and is widely employed by security services and companies responsible for the safety of high-ranking officials.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The MP9 submachine gun was developed by the Swiss company BT AG (Brugger & Thomet AG) and was first presented to the public in 2001. This weapon is primarily designed for military and police use, although a semi-automatic version has been created based on it for civilian and police use. The Brugger & Thomet MP9 submachine gun can use magazines with capacities of 15, 20, 25, and 30 rounds. It features three safeties: a fire mode selector, a trigger safety, and an automatic drop safety. It is a further development of the Steyr TMP, acquired from Steyr in 2001. It differs from the TMP with a side-folding stock, a trigger safety, and an integrated Picatinny rail.
 
The primary users of the Swiss MP9 submachine gun are special police units and security services. The weapon is suitable for conducting assault operations, hostage rescues, for concealed carry, and for use in small enclosed spaces (preferably without a suppressor). Its compact size is especially effective in scenarios where it can be easily concealed under clothing, making the submachine gun inconspicuous and blending with the silhouette of the person carrying it.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Switzerland
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2001-present

%c[0,153,255,255]FN P90%c[0,140,140,140] - a Belgian submachine gun (personal defense weapon), developed by FN Herstal in 1986-1987. It was primarily designed for tank crews and vehicle drivers.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The FN P90 is built in a bullpup layout, operating with a free-floating bolt. It fires from a closed bolt. It has a hammer-fired trigger mechanism, allowing both single-shot and automatic fire (in fixed 3-round bursts). The magazine is mounted above the barrel and holds 50 rounds, with cartridges positioned perpendicular to the barrel and rotating 90 degrees before entering the chamber. The magazine is made of transparent plastic, allowing the shooter to see the remaining rounds. Spent casings are ejected downwards through a hollow fire control grip. The FN P90 is equipped with an integrated 1X magnification collimator sight with a reticle in the shape of a T. One of the main design goals was to minimize the weight of the weapon and ammunition. This weapon-ammunition complex was intended to potentially replace traditional 9mm pistols and submachine guns in NATO armies.
 
Specifically for the P90, the 5.7x28mm SS190 cartridge was developed, known for its high penetration power and low ricochet characteristics. The bullet of this cartridge achieves a muzzle velocity of up to 715 m/s and is capable of penetrating a titanium/kevlar body armor meeting NATO CRISAT standards at distances up to 20 meters.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Belgium
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1991-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Silent Pistol (PB)%c[0,140,140,140] was created to equip army reconnaissance groups, as well as KGB USSR personnel, and was adopted in 1967.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The PB (index 6P9), equipped with an integrated and removable suppressor, was created by A. A. Deryagin at TsNII Tochmash. The PB is a standalone system due to numerous changes and reworkings of the weapon mechanisms compared to the PM. Since the slide is significantly shortened, the return spring is placed vertically in the handle, interacting through a rocking two-arm lever with the slide. Unlike most similar systems, the sound suppressor is made of two detachable parts. This solution allows the pistol to be carried and stored with the front part of the suppressor (attachment) removed, and before use, the attachment can be quickly installed on the weapon. At the same time, the pistol retains the ability to be safely fired by the shooter with the attachment removed, which is important in critical situations. Naturally, with the attachment removed, the sound of the shot is as loud as that of a regular PM pistol. But even with the attachment installed, the pistol does not become completely silent, as the shooter's position is revealed by the clanking of the slide.
 
Despite the massive suppressor, the PB has good balance. The pistol is known for its great durability and longevity. There are known examples with a round count several times exceeding the warranty, and still having no serious defects. Currently, the PB continues to be in service with FSB special units and internal troops of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Russian Federation, but is gradually being replaced by the PSS pistol. However, since 2017, the PB pistol has once again become the weapon of all officers of reconnaissance and special forces units (SpN). There is a persistent belief that the PB was made based on the Makarov pistol, but this is not the case, although it was indeed ordered to use the trigger mechanism and magazine from the PM.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1967-1989 (2003-present)

%c[0,153,255,255]Tul'skiy Tokareva (TT)%c[0,140,140,140] - self-loading pistol, developed in 1930 by Soviet designer Fyodor Vasilyevich Tokarev. The first self-loading pistol of Soviet design, adopted for service in the USSR.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The TT pistol was developed for the 1929 competition for a new army pistol, announced with the goal of replacing the "nagan" revolver and several models of foreign-made revolvers and pistols that were in service with the Red Army by the mid-1920s. The standard cartridge adopted was the German 7.63x25mm Mauser cartridge, which was purchased in significant quantities for the Mauser C96 pistols in service. Several thousand units were produced between 1930 and 1932. In early 1934, the new pistol was adopted for service under the designation "7.62mm self-loading pistol model 1933." After the war, the Tokarev pistol gained popularity in criminal circles due to its availability, driven by large stocks in military depots and thus a low price on the black market, as well as the fact that many units of this weapon were not entered into the gun registry, which made identifying individual weapons and tracing their history of use difficult.
 
The TT pistol is distinguished by its simple design, which results in low production costs and ease of maintenance. The atypical, very powerful cartridge provides unusually high penetration and muzzle energy of about 500 Joules. The pistol has a short, light trigger pull and provides significant shooting accuracy; an experienced shooter can hit a target at distances of more than 50 meters. The pistol is flat and compact, making it convenient for concealed carry. However, there were drawbacks noted during its operation. The TT pistol is characterized by the flatness of its trajectory and the high penetration of its sharp-pointed bullet, which can penetrate army helmets or lightweight body armor. The penetration of the TT bullet exceeds that of the 9x19mm Parabellum cartridge.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140] 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1930-1953

%c[0,153,255,255]PKM%c[0,140,140,140] - a model of group, portable, man-portable, or stationary automatic small arms for the 7.62mm rifle machine gun cartridge (7.62x54), a part of the unified machine gun small arms complex.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The PKM machine gun was created in the late 1960s based on the design of the unified machine gun PK at the Izhmash Design Bureau under the leadership of Mikhail Kalashnikov and adopted into service in 1969 under the designation "7.62mm modernized Kalashnikov machine gun, PKM," index 6P6M. Serial production of the PKM machine gun and its modifications was carried out by the Kovrov Mechanical Plant until the liquidation of its firearms production. Since 2006, the machine gun has been produced at the V.A. Degtyaryov Plant (Kovrov). The task of modernizing the machine gun complex was to reduce the weight of the machine gun and its mount. During the modernization, the barrel, barrel jacket, barrel cover, bolt frame, gas piston tube, receiver base, and cartridge boxes were lightened. The shape of the stock was also changed, and a new flash hider was developed. The stock received a new butt plate and folding shoulder rest.
 
The 7.62mm PKM machine gun is designed to engage personnel, including those in individual body armor, unarmored and lightly armored weapons, enemy military and special equipment at distances up to 1500 meters. The most effective fire is conducted at distances up to 1000 meters. The machine gun provides high-density fire at close distances and can engage important group and individual distant targets. Fire from the machine gun is conducted in short (up to 10 rounds) or long (up to 30 rounds) bursts or in bursts of arbitrary length.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Light machine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1969-present

%c[0,153,255,255]9mm Makarov Pistol (PM)%c[0,140,140,140] - a Soviet semi-automatic pistol, developed by Soviet designer Nikolay Fyodorovich Makarov in 1948. Adopted into service in 1951. It is a personal weapon for assault and defense, designed to engage the enemy at short distances, used in armed forces, law enforcement agencies, and one of the most widely used pistols in the USSR and the post-Soviet space.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Despite the apparent simplicity of the PM pistol's design, significant difficulties arose during its production. The designer's efforts to reduce the number of parts led to the necessity of making them multifunctional, and therefore quite complex in terms of their geometry. This complexity defined the difficulty of producing the pistol. Initially, the percentage of defects in pistol production was very high. At that time, specialists referred to the PM pistol as an example of "non-technological." Achieving high reliability of the pistol and its manufacturability was possible only due to the significant efforts of designers and technologists at the Izhevsk Mechanical Plant. Development and application of new technological processes, the use of modern equipment ensured mass production of the PM pistol.
 
The pistol is designed to engage open enemy forces in close combat. All types of 9mm pistol cartridges (9x18) are used for firing from the pistol. Currently, the PM pistol is in service with all power ministries and departments of Russia. In the Russian army, the PM pistol is the primary personal weapon of officers and certain categories of servicemen. In the early 21st century, to enhance the combat capabilities of the PM pistol, 9mm pistol cartridges with armor-piercing bullets (2003) and 9mm pistol cartridges with improved ballistic characteristics (2005) were developed and adopted.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
