%c[0,153,255,255]9mm Automatic Pistol Stechkin (APS)%c[0,140,140,140] - an automatic pistol developed in the late 1940s to early 1950s by designer I. Ya. Stechkin and adopted by the Armed Forces of the USSR in 1951, simultaneously with the Makarov pistol. The APS is intended for arming officers actively engaged in combat, as well as soldiers and sergeants of certain special units.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The pistol's development was led by young engineer Igor Yakovlevich Stechkin, who joined the Central Design Bureau-14 in 1948 and presented the first prototype in 1949. After project approval, factory tests, a series of improvements, and military trials, the pistol was adopted into service. The weapon could fire both single shots and bursts. The APS pistol was intended for officers, sergeants, soldiers of specific specialties, and vehicle crews who were not equipped with a standard-issue rifle or carbine. It was believed that the PM pistol would not be sufficient for self-defense in combat situations. In 1958, the APS was discontinued, and by the 1960s, the majority of military pistols were in storage (although it remained in service with certain categories of servicemen, particularly with RPG-7 grenadiers and PK machine gunners until the early 1980s). At the same time, the APS, which had better accuracy, less recoil, less muzzle rise during firing, and slightly higher firepower than the PM, continued to be used by the KGB of the USSR and by servicemen of certain specialties of the GRU Spetsnaz.
 
After the dissolution of the USSR, the pistol was used in various local conflicts. The APSB (AO-44, product 6P13) - a "silent" model with a silencer, developed in the early 1970s by designer A. S. Neugodov and adopted into service in 1972.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)

%c[0,153,255,255]Armsel Protecta%c[0,140,140,140], also known as the Sentinel Arms Co Striker-12, Protecta, Protecta Bulldog, and SWD Street Sweeper, is a 12-gauge combat shotgun with a revolving cylinder, designed for riot control and combat.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The weapon was developed by Hilton Walker of Rhodesia in the early 1980s. The first samples of the combat shotgun were produced in the mid-1980s in South Africa. The weapon was notable for its large magazine capacity and high practical rate of fire. On the other hand, the drum magazine was heavy, cumbersome, and the weapon had several mechanical issues. For these reasons, Walker improved his design in the late 1980s, naming the new shotgun Protecta. Currently, the Protecta shotgun is manufactured in South Africa by Reutech Defense Industries and is used both domestically and exported.
 
The Protecta shotgun is built on a revolver-type scheme, with a self-cocking hammer-fired mechanism and fed from a non-detachable rotating drum with 12 rounds. Reloading the shotgun is done one round at a time through a window in the rear end of the drum housing.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: South Africa
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1980s

%c[0,153,255,255]AA-12 (Auto Assault-12)%c[0,140,140,140] - automatic shotgun, developed by Maxwell Atchisson based on an earlier design, the Atchisson Assault Shotgun.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The AAS shotgun was developed by Atchisson based on the experience gained during the Vietnam War, which demonstrated the effectiveness of shotguns in close combat in the jungle. One way to increase firepower was to automate the reloading process, so after the war, several similar systems began development. In the early 1980s, the system was upgraded, resulting in the AA-12 shotgun. Over the next 20 years, Atchisson continued to refine his design, which never went into production. In the 2000s, the American company Military Police Systems acquired the rights to the AA-12, hoping to begin production for police and the U.S. armed forces. It was not made available for commercial sale to civilians. Additionally, using Atchisson's ideas, the automatic shotgun USAS-12 was developed in South Korea.
 
The main difference between the AA-12 and its predecessor is the use of gas-operated automatics, typical of assault rifles and machine guns, allowing for the use of more powerful cartridges. Different magazines were designed for the AA-12. The box magazine now holds 8 cartridges, while the drum magazine still holds 20. The dimensions of the shotgun also changed, making it shorter at 965 mm, while the length of the barrel remained unchanged. Another feature of the shotgun is "recoil impulse accumulation". This involves the interaction of two return springs, stretching the recoil impulse over time. In 2004, 10 AA-12 prototypes were created and demonstrated to the United States Marine Corps.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: not mass-produced

%c[0,153,255,255]RG-6 Gnome%c[0,140,140,140] (index GRAU - 6G30) - hand-held revolver grenade launcher.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Developed in 1993 at TsKIB SOO by designers Valery Telesh and V. A. Borzov. Small-scale production of the RG-6 began in 1994 at the Tula Arms Plant, and the first batch of six grenade launchers was delivered for testing in the military in the first quarter of 1994. It was used in Chechnya and Georgia. The RG-6 was clearly influenced by the South African Milkor MGL grenade launcher, but it uses fundamentally different ammunition with a flying casing, which results in several noticeable differences in its design. The RG-6 design is characterized by extreme simplicity and technological efficiency. The RG-6 grenade launcher has a revolver scheme, with the chambers of the cylinder formed by six rifled muzzle-loading barrels from GP-25 grenade launchers. Accordingly, the loading of the cylinder is carried out from the front, after folding the front part of the grenade launcher with the false barrel to the side and up. Grenades are held in the chambers by special spring-loaded fixators. The rotation of the cylinder is carried out by a spring manually wound during reloading.
 
The self-cocking firing mechanism is mainly borrowed from the GP-25 grenade launcher. The grenade launcher is equipped with a false barrel that has a smooth (non-rifled) channel and serves only to mount the front handle and sight. The sights are folding, of a step type. The telescopic stock is collapsible and has a rubber shock-absorbing pad. Due to the rushed development, early models of the grenade launcher had some problems, including unreliable operation of the firing mechanism.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1994-present

%c[0,153,255,255]RGD-5%c[0,140,140,140] - Soviet offensive hand grenade, belongs to the anti-personnel fragmentation hand grenades of remote-action offensive type.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The name of the grenade, RGD-5, stands for "hand grenade, remote-action, 5th model". The grenade is designed to strike enemy personnel with the explosive effect of the filler and fragments formed when the metal casing is destroyed. The target is reached by throwing the grenade by hand. "Remote-action" means that the grenade will explode after a certain time (3.2-4.2 seconds) from the moment it is released, regardless of other conditions. "Offensive type" means that the grenade's fragments have a small mass and fly a shorter distance than the possible throwing range.
 
In 1954, the RGD-5 was adopted by the Soviet Armed Forces, replacing the RG-42 offensive grenade. The grenades were used in combat operations in Syria. The combat grenade is painted in an olive-gray color.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)

%c[0,153,255,255]RGM-40 Kastet%c[0,140,140,140] stands for "handheld grenade launcher, compact caliber 40 mm". The grenade launcher is designed to fire caseless grenades VOG-25, VOG-25P, and other modifications against enemy personnel and unarmored targets. The grenade launcher was created in 1997 by the weapons designer V.N. Telesh for the units of the Ministry of Internal Affairs.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The RGM-40 grenade launcher is a single-shot grenade launcher based on the underbarrel grenade launcher GP-30 "Obuvka". Unlike the GP-30, the RGM-40 does not have the capability to be mounted under a rifle; the mount under the rifle is replaced by a tube into which the buttstock is folded, essentially making it an autonomous grenade launcher. The grenade launcher consists of a rifled barrel, which is loaded through the muzzle. The grenade launcher can be discharged by pressing the ejection mechanism. It has a plastic pistol grip borrowed from the Kalashnikov Assault Rifle. The self-cocking firing mechanism fires after pressing the trigger and breaking the cap. The telescopic buttstock is used to reduce dimensions. A rubber pad is placed on the buttstock to reduce recoil force.
 
The 40 mm handheld grenade launcher RGM "Kastet" is designed to incapacitate enemy personnel without lethal consequences using special ammunition (tear gas, impact, etc.), as well as to engage open and in trench positions, reverse slopes, and other unarmored targets inaccessible to small arms with fragmentation ammunition VOG-25 and VOG-25P by direct and indirect fire. The grenade launcher did not become popular because it lacks military purpose since a standard underbarrel grenade launcher can be used.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia

%c[0,153,255,255]RPG-7%c[0,140,140,140] - Soviet reusable handheld anti-tank grenade launcher.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The RPG-7 is a light recoilless rocket launcher (without recoil upon firing). The design of the launcher and the projectile is based on the proven RPG-2 scheme of a reusable recoilless launcher and a shot with a super-caliber warhead. The grenade launcher consists of a barrel with optical and mechanical sights, a trigger mechanism with a safety catch, and a firing mechanism. The barrel of the grenade launcher consists of a tube and a nozzle, which directs the flight of the grenade and diverts the powder gases upon firing. Unlike the RPG-2, the RPG-7 grenade launcher has an expanded barrel in its middle part - a charge chamber - for more complete use of the throwing charge energy, and a nozzle in the breech part - to ensure the recoillessness of the system.
 
It is designed to combat tanks, self-propelled artillery installations, and other armored vehicles, and can be used to destroy enemy personnel in cover, as well as to engage low-flying, low-speed aerial targets. The RPG-7 was developed by the State Instrument Design Bureau (GSKB-47, now GNPP "Bazalt") and was adopted by the Armed Forces of the Soviet Union in 1961. The chief designer was Valentin Konstantinovich Firulin. Over 9 million units have been produced. It has been used in almost all armed conflicts since 1968 and remains in service with the armed forces of many countries and states. Due to the introduction of new ammunition, the RPG-7 remains a significant threat to modern weaponry and military equipment, and therefore remains in demand in the 21st century.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]RPK-74%c[0,140,140,140] - a hand-held machine gun developed to replace the RPK machine gun in the Soviet army, chambered for the 7.62x39mm cartridge, within the framework of the small-caliber 5.45x39mm rifle ammunition system.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The serial production of the RPK-74 machine gun and its modifications was carried out by the Molot Machine Building Plant in Vyatskiye Polyany from 1975 to 1993. The standardization of components and parts of the RPK-74 machine gun with the AK-74 assault rifle not only simplified its production but also facilitated its introduction and use in the army. The 5.45mm RPK-74 machine gun and its modifications are the most powerful automatic small arms in a motorized rifle unit and are designed to engage enemy personnel, unarmored weapon samples, military and special equipment. All types of 5.45mm rifle cartridges (5.45x39) are used for firing from the RPK-74 machine gun and its modifications. The RPK-74 machine gun complex provides high-density fire at short distances and can engage important group and single distant targets. Fire from the machine gun is conducted in short bursts (up to 5 shots) or long bursts (up to 15 shots).
 
Adopted into service in 1974 together with the AK-74 assault rifle. In 1993, after the creation of the AK-74M assault rifle, corresponding modifications were made to the RPK-74 at the Izhevsk Machine Building Plant and the Vyatskiye Polyany Machine Building Plant "Molot", following the principle of standardization, to bring it up to the RPK-74M level. For export, based on the RPK-74M, a variant RPK-201 chambered for the 5.56x45mm NATO cartridge was also developed, using magazines from the AK-101 of the same caliber, and the RPK-203 chambered for the 7.62x39mm cartridge.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1974-present

%c[0,153,255,255]RPK-16%c[0,140,140,140] - a Russian hand-held machine gun chambered in 5.45x39mm. It is an initiative development by the Kalashnikov Concern.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
As per the customer's requirements, the RPK-16 was developed as a domestic equivalent of foreign 5.56x45mm caliber machine guns such as the M249 and FN Minimi, combining high firepower with sufficient mobility, the ability for quick barrel change, and the use of both belt-fed and box magazines (NATO standard for assault rifles). However, the developers of the RPK-16 opted against dual feeding, instead retaining the option to use magazines compatible with the AK-74 / RPK-74. The RPK-16 has a layout similar to the AK-12 but with improved ergonomics. The weight of the machine gun is approximately 4.5 kg. The RPK-16 also retains Kalashnikov's classic long-stroke gas piston system, ensuring high reliability. With a low recoil coefficient, the RPK-16 has high shooting accuracy and noticeably exceeds the firepower of the RPK-74. Its rate of fire is up to 700 rounds per minute. The machine gun can fire in single shots or bursts with a closed bolt. Thanks to Picatinny rail guides on the top and bottom of the handguard, any necessary additional equipment can be quickly installed, including modern sighting systems. The machine gun can be equipped with a folding bipod.
 
This machine gun is being developed as a potential replacement for the Kalashnikov RPK-74 machine gun, both in regular troops and special forces units. The weapon is based on the solutions obtained during the research and development work on the AK-12 assault rifle and the RPK-400 machine gun. According to the statement of the Kalashnikov Concern expert Sergey Radkevich, the machine gun incorporates the experience of modern armed conflicts. The RPK-16 was first presented at the "Army-2017" forum. The designation RPK-16 stands for "Ruchnoy Pulemyot Kalashnikova model of 2016".
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]SA-58 (FN FAL)%c[0,140,140,140] - NATO firearm manufactured in Belgium by Fabrique Nationale de Herstal. One of the most recognized and widely used automatic rifles.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The FN FAL was initially developed as a weapon for medium-range combat. The replacement of the intermediate cartridge 7.92x33mm with the 7.62x51mm NATO cartridge led to an increase in muzzle energy and consequently extended the effective range of the weapon. As a result, the model was capable of effectively firing single shots at distances up to 1000 meters and providing satisfactory accuracy with short bursts at distances not exceeding 100-200 meters. Alongside the Kalashnikov and M16 rifles, the FN FAL is one of the most widely used automatic firearms in the world. It is widely used worldwide and has been in service in armies of more than 90 countries in various modifications. The FN FAL has gained wide popularity worldwide, and there are numerous different modifications in various countries.
 
The SA-58 OSW (Operations Specialist Weapon) is manufactured by the American company DSA (David Selveggio Arms) on the same production line as the StG-58 and is an official copy of the "FAL" rifles. The civilian version is only produced in a semi-automatic variant, while a modification is also available for police or military units with the ability to fire fully automatic. The DSA-58 is fully compatible with any metric magazines for the FN FAL.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]Saiga-12K%c[0,140,140,140] - a semi-automatic shotgun of 12 gauge, produced at the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant based on the Kalashnikov assault rifle, designed for commercial and amateur hunting of small and medium-sized game and birds in areas with any climatic conditions.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The smoothbore Saiga shotguns inherited the general layout and design of the AK, with a gas-operated mechanism and rotating bolt lock. The bolt group and barrel housing were redesigned for the use of hunting cartridges, the trigger mechanism was deprived of an automatic release, and a gas regulator was added to the gas-operated mechanism to ensure reliable firing with both regular and magnum cartridges. In addition, some models are equipped with Picatinny rails, allowing for the use of additional equipment (collimator sights, flashlights, etc.).
 
The "Saiga-12K" is a shortened semi-automatic shotgun - a "smoothbore carbine". The stock and pistol grip (and in some versions, the handguard) are borrowed from the Kalashnikov assault rifle. The design includes a locking mechanism that prevents the weapon from being taken off safety until the stock is folded. The barrel length is 430 mm. Sights are of hunting or "rifle" type.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]FN SCAR%c[0,140,140,140] - a firearm system developed by the American division of the Belgian company FN Herstal for the competition to create a new rifle for USSOCOM fighters in 2004.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
In December 2004, this system was declared the winner of the competition, and rifles are currently being supplied to US Rangers. In July 2007, the US Armed Forces conducted testing of new assault rifles intended to replace the M4 carbine. The test included the German HK416, Belgian FN SCAR, and the joint German-American XM8. Most of the testing was conducted in desert conditions. The FN SCAR rifle showed the best results in the tests. On November 13, 2008, the United States officially announced that it would be adopted. In 2010, the FN SCAR was adopted by units serving in Afghanistan. In 2013, USSOCOM discontinued further purchases of the SCAR-L and plans to dispose of the remaining rifles. Instead, they will be purchasing SCAR-H with a conversion kit for the 5.56 mm cartridge, compensating for the lack of SCAR-L.
 
The SCAR rifle system includes two basic weapon variants - the "light" rifle Mk.16 SCAR-L (Light) and the "heavy" rifle Mk.17 SCAR-H (Heavy). The main differences between the SCAR-L and SCAR-H are the ammunition used - SCAR-L rifles are designed only for 5.56x45 mm NATO cartridges (both standard M855 bullets and heavier Mk.262 bullets). SCAR-H rifles use the much more powerful 7.62x51 mm NATO cartridge as their base ammunition, with the possibility, after replacing necessary components (bolt, barrel, lower part of the barrel housing with the magazine receiver), of using other cartridges (though these plans have not yet been realized). In both basic configurations, SCAR rifles are intended to have three possible configurations - standard "S" (Standard), shortened for close combat "CQC" (Close Quarters Combat), and sniper "SV" (Sniper Variant).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]SIG Sauer P220%c[0,140,140,140] - a pistol developed in the mid-1960s and produced by the Swiss-German company SIG Sauer.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The development of a new army pistol for the Swiss Army to replace the very high-quality but also very expensive and morally outdated SIG P210 pistol began in the early 1970s. The well-known Swiss company SIG developed such a pistol, and in the mid-1970s it was adopted under the designation Pistole 75. The pistol undoubtedly had great export potential, but due to strict restrictions imposed by Swiss legislation, its export was limited. Therefore, SIG entered into an alliance with the well-known German firearms manufacturer J.P. Sauer and Sohns, which allowed the production of pistols commercially designated SIG-Sauer P220 in Germany. Over time, all calibers were phased out of the P220 series except .45 ACP, in which the P220 continues to be produced to this day. The 9mm P220 pistol is in service with the armies of Switzerland and Japan, while the .45 ACP version is widely used in various police departments in the USA. Currently, a whole range of modifications of the P220 pistol in .45 caliber is produced, aimed at both the civilian and law enforcement markets.
 
SIG-Sauer P220 pistols, like all subsequent SIG-Sauer developments, are rightly renowned for their high reliability, good ergonomics, and excellent shooting accuracy. This weapon is quite popular among security agencies and police departments in the USA, as well as among special forces units in many countries around the world. SIG-Sauer P220R is a modification with a rail for mounting a laser sight.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,153,255,255]SIG SG 550/Stgw 90%c[0,140,140,140] - Swiss assault rifle developed in 1979-1980 based on the SIG SG 540 to replace the outdated SIG SG 510 battle rifle.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The SIG SG 550 rifle was developed based on its predecessors, the SIG SG 540 and SG 541, with major modifications aimed at lightening the weapon. In 1983, the SIG SG 550 was officially adopted by the Swiss Army under the designation Stgw.90, but production began only in 1986 due to funding issues. Later, based on the SG 550, the SG 551 carbine and the SG 552 compact assault rifle were developed, as well as their civilian versions that cannot fire in automatic mode. Overall, the SIG SG 550 family of rifles is one of the best in its class, known for high reliability, shooting accuracy, and ease of use. The SG 550 can be equipped with a bayonet and an optical sight mount. In the Swiss Army, Stgw.90 rifles are sometimes equipped with a 4X scope, while export versions of all models can be equipped with commercial optical or reflex ("red dot") sights. Modern production SG 550 rifles may come with a Picatinny rail on the top surface of the receiver, in place of or in addition to open sights.
 
The magazines for the classic SG 550 are original, made of semi-transparent plastic and are interlocked like G36 magazines. The standard magazines in the Swiss Army hold 20 rounds, but there are also 30-round and larger capacity magazines. Civilian magazines hold 10 rounds. The model 556 has a lower receiver magazine well adapted for standard M-16 magazines. This model is particularly popular in America. The rifle is quite popular in the United States. The SIG SG 552 Commando is an even more compact rifle with a 226 mm barrel, designed for special police units. Unlike the SG 550, the recoil spring is behind the bolt carrier and acts directly on it, rather than on the gas piston rod. The rifle has a modified flash hider and can be equipped with Picatinny rails for mounting additional accessories.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Switzerland
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1990-present

%c[0,153,255,255]HK416%c[0,140,140,140] - German assault rifle adapted to the American modular AR-15 system, created by Heckler & Koch. Despite its external similarity to the M4 carbine, the rifle mechanism is actually closer to the German HK G36, rather than the AR pattern of American rifles.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Heckler & Koch decided to enter the market of US military and police weapons with a new alternative system designated as the HK416. This modular assault rifle combines the familiar ergonomics and appearance of the M16 rifle for any American with significantly improved reliability, achieved through a number of measures. First and foremost, this includes replacing the direct gas impingement system of the M16 rifle with a more reliable (according to the manufacturer) and significantly less sensitive to contamination gas piston system, with a short stroke, borrowed from the G36 rifle. Initially, the HK416 was developed as a separate interchangeable module for installation on any M16 or M4 carbine lower receiver, but later, HK began producing complete HK416 carbines.
 
Currently, the HK-416 series rifles are in service with special units of many countries. The name HK416 indicates compatibility with NATO standards for magazines from the M4 and M16. The latest variant of the rifle is named HK416A5. Additionally, there is a version chambered in 7.62x51 mm - the HK417, which competes with the FN SCAR. It has been adopted by the Bundeswehr under the designation G38 family.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany/USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2005-present

%c[0,153,255,255]7.62mm Simonov self-loading carbine (SKS)%c[0,140,140,140] - Soviet self-loading carbine designed by Sergei Simonov, adopted into service in 1949.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
These carbines are very popular among hunters and gamekeepers due to their low cost, ease of maintenance, simplicity, and reliability. The carbine operates reliably at temperatures from -50 to +50 °C. It is generally considered that these SKS variants are mainly used for shooting at fast and relatively small animals such as wolves or foxes, since the 7.62x39 mm cartridge is not effective for large game. By the end of the 1950s, in order to standardize small arms in the Soviet Army, a decision was made to replace carbines with rifles. Nevertheless, the SKS remained in service with air defense forces until the early 1990s, as well as with a number of engineering and military construction units and supply units where engaging in gunfire was not the main task. Considering the huge number of SKS carbines currently in storage, there are several enterprises in the Russian Federation, Ukraine, Belarus, and other former Soviet republics that convert SKS carbines into OP-SKS and "Arkhar" models, after which these carbines are sold in the civilian market. The primary market for civilian SKS carbines worldwide is the United States, where a large industry has developed for producing accessories for this carbine. Detachable magazines, plastic stocks, and optic mounts are manufactured.
 
Due to the almost complete absence of protruding elements, the carbine is convenient for performing precise military maneuvers with the weapon. Additionally, in the Russian Federation and several other countries, it is allowed for sale to civilians with the necessary documents and is mainly used by hunters. OP-SKS (hunting and gamekeeping) - SKS, converted at the "Molot" factory or the Tula Arms Plant from combat to hunting weapons by removing the bayonet mount, limiting the sight mount with a pin to 300 meters, and applying new markings (in accordance with the Federal Law of the Russian Federation "On Weapons"). Otherwise, the SKS and OP-SKS are completely identical.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: self-loading carbine
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1949-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Franchi SPAS-12%c[0,140,140,140] (Special Purpose Automatic Shotgun) - Italian shotgun developed in the late 1970s.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Franchi SPAS-12 is a dual-mode shotgun, meaning it can be used as a semi-automatic or pump-action weapon. Switching between firing modes is done by pressing a button under the barrel and moving the forend slightly forward or backward until it clicks. One of the unique features of the SPAS-12 is a switch visible on the folding stock of the shotgun. This switch can be rotated 90 degrees so that the shotgun fits under the shooter's forearm. Theoretically, holding the shotgun by the pistol grip, the shooter can fire one-handed: from the hip or with an extended arm; however, due to the weight of the shotgun, this is highly unlikely. Despite its heavy barrel, the SPAS-12 has the lowest recoil of all 12-gauge shotguns. A grenade launcher can be attached to the barrel, with a firing range of up to 150 meters.
 
The first and most common variant of the SPAS-12 had a metal folding stock and an eight-round magazine. A small number of early models had a detachable wooden stock. After import restrictions were introduced in the United States in 1989, a version with a plastic fixed stock and a five or six-round magazine was released to comply with the required restrictions. The successor to the SPAS-12 was the semi-automatic/pump-action shotgun Franchi SPAS-15.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: semi-automatic shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Italy
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1979-2000

%c[0,153,255,255]Benelli M4 Super 90%c[0,140,140,140] - smoothbore semi-automatic magazine shotgun developed in Italy by Benelli Armi S.p.A..
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Developed in Italy in 1998. It is used by military and law enforcement agencies of various countries, as well as by civilians worldwide. It is known for its high reliability. The shotgun operates using gas diverted from the barrel through two symmetrically placed gas cylinders under the barrel. The military version (designated as the US Army M1014) has two firing modes - semi-automatic and manual pump action - and a 6-round magazine. Various collimator and electronic sights can be installed on the firearm. The civilian version lacks the fire mode selector and has a 4-round magazine.
 
On the Russian civilian market, the Benelli M4 Super 90 semi-automatic shotgun (smoothbore) is sold in a configuration completely identical to the American military M1014 shotgun. Accessories and additional equipment are produced for Benelli M4 shotguns, including tactical flashlights (including those integrated into the forearm), folding and telescopic stocks, and tactical slings, among others.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: semi-automatic shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Italy
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1998-present

%c[0,153,255,255]SR-1%c[0,140,140,140] Samozaryadnyy Pistolet Serdyukova (SPS, "Gyurza", GRAU Index 6P53) - a pistol developed for special forces units under the NIIOKR "Grach" program by the designers P.I. Serdyukov and I.V. Belyaev at TsNIITochMash for the 9x21mm high-efficiency cartridge, introduced in 1995.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The need for a pistol radically different from those in service with armies, law enforcement agencies, special services, and special forces units arose in the 1980s. This was due to the widespread introduction of various classes of personal body armor into the armed forces of most countries. For example, a second-class bulletproof vest can stop 7.62mm bullets with a steel core fired from a TT pistol at close range, and 9mm bullets of the standard NATO cartridge fired from a Beretta 92FS pistol. The pistol was developed to replace the APS used in special forces of law enforcement agencies. Tactical and technical requirements included the ability to accurately shoot at distances up to 100 meters, to penetrate personal body armor, and to disable automobiles and other unarmored vehicles.
 
Variants and modifications: the SR1M pistol - serially produced for the FSO and FSB since 2003. It is used with various types of ammunition, including expanding bullets. It has a handle with an enlarged safety lever. Button magazine release and frame like the SPS. Sights have been modified. After replacing the magazine, the bolt automatically disengages from the bolt hold open. The SR1MP pistol - the latest modification, featuring slots on the sides of the front part of the frame to attach a Picatinny rail for mounting various additional accessories: collimator sights, laser target designators (LTD), and tactical flashlights. A suppressor can be installed on the SR1MP pistol for silent shooting.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1996-present

%c[0,153,255,255]SR-2 "Veresk"%c[0,140,140,140] ("special development 2") - a Russian submachine gun designed for close combat (up to 200 m) against enemy personnel using individual body armor.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Development of a new submachine gun for the 9x21mm high-efficiency cartridge began in the mid-1990s under the order of the Federal Security Service (FSB) at the Klimovsk Central Research Institute for Tochmash. The new model was presented in 1999 under the provisional designation SR-2. Along with the SR-1 "Gyurza" pistol, SP-10 and SP-11 cartridges, and the unified collimator sight "KP SR-2", this submachine gun is part of an enhanced efficiency firearms complex designed for special units. "Veresk" was originally designed as a weapon capable of engaging enemies in second-class body armor and unarmored vehicles at distances up to 200 m. These requirements determined the choice of ammunition. The SR-2 ensures 100% penetration of a 4 mm steel sheet at a distance of 100 m. The "Veresk" submachine gun is adopted by the FSB (including the "Vympel" unit) and the FSO of Russia, as well as some special units of the Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs (including the Moscow OMON).
 
Variants and modifications: SR-2 "Veresk" - the original model, no longer in production. SR-2M "Veresk" - a modernized model: a folding front grip appeared on the barrel instead of a rigid rest, improving weapon control and shooting accuracy (in the folded position, the grip becomes part of the barrel). The safety lever has been slightly modified, and the muzzle device is now a muzzle brake-compensator, protecting the shooter's hand from forward displacement and powder gas burns. SR-2MP "Veresk" - an improved version of the SR-2M "Veresk" with Picatinny rails on the top cover of the receiver and barrel. It is now possible to install a suppressor and a modified form stock.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1999-present

%c[0,153,255,255]SV-98%c[0,140,140,140] - a Russian magazine-fed sniper rifle, developed between 1998 and 2000 by a team of designers under the leadership of Vladimir Dmitrievich Stronsky and serially produced by the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant (now Kalashnikov Concern). The SV-98 is designed for hitting targets at distances up to 1000 meters.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
This rifle is built on a classic scheme typical for such weapons: a longitudinally sliding bolt locks on three locking lugs; after firing, the bolt must be manually opened to extract the cartridge case and closed to chamber the next round. The rifle features a sporting type trigger mechanism. The stock is made of modified wood, with a notch for folding a bipod in the front and a handle in the middle for carrying the weapon. The cheek rest and butt plate are adjustable. It also comes equipped with telescopic bipods, an anti-mirage band for the barrel that eliminates image distortion caused by heated air during firing, a suppressor, and a 1P69 Hyperon panoramic sight. The rifle allows effective firing at distances of 700-800 meters. Currently, a modernized version of the SV-98 with an aluminum stock, foldable adjustable butt stock, and a Picatinny rail on the receiver for mounting optical and night sights is serially produced by the Kalashnikov Concern.
 
An experienced shooter with this type of rifle can hit a human-sized target at a distance of 1200 meters. Comparable rifles include the American Remington 700, the German Blaser 93, and similar rifles. In Russia, it is used by special units of the Ministry of Defense, Ministry of Justice, Ministry of Internal Affairs, Federal Security Service, and Federal Protective Service. In December 2012, an order was placed for 49 units for the special forces of the Ministry of Internal Affairs (Department for Control of Narcotics Trafficking - UKON). By early August 2015, the SV-98 became the main type of sniper rifle in the airborne troops. In 2013, Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant modernized the rifle. Unlike its predecessor, the SV-98M received a new stock made of a duralumin alloy.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2004-present

%c[0,153,255,255]7.62mm Sniper Rifle Dragunov (SVD)%c[0,140,140,140] - a Soviet semi-automatic sniper rifle, developed between 1957 and 1963 by a team of designers led by Yevgeny Dragunov, and adopted by the Soviet Army on July 3, 1963, together with the PSO-1 optical sight.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The SVD is used with 7.62x54mm R rifle cartridges, including standard, tracer, armor-piercing incendiary bullets, sniper cartridges 7N1, and sniper armor-piercing 7N14. It can also fire cartridges with JHP and JSP expanding bullets. The SVD fires single shots from a detachable box magazine with a capacity of 10 rounds. A five-slot muzzle brake on the muzzle reduces flash and protects against barrel fouling. A gas regulator adjusts the recoil speed of moving parts, ensuring the rifle's reliability. The SVD was also produced in small quantities with a tactical suppressor-muzzle brake known as TGP-V, developed by NPO Special Equipment and Communication, which mounts over the standard muzzle brake, although its effectiveness was disputed.
 
The PSO-1 sight is calibrated for shooting up to 1300 meters. It is generally considered that effective shooting at such distances is only possible against group targets or for harassment fire. However, in 1985, in Afghanistan, sniper Vladimir Ilyin of the 345th Guards Airborne Regiment hit an enemy target at a distance of 1350 meters. This remains a record not only for the SVD but also for 7.62mm caliber rifles in general. The SVD-S is a variant of the SVD designed for airborne troops with a folding stock and a shortened but thicker barrel, created in 1991 and adopted in 1995.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1963-present

%c[0,153,255,255]SVU (Self-loading Sniper Rifle, Shortened)%c[0,140,140,140] (OЦ-03) - a bullpup configuration self-loading sniper rifle developed by TsKIB SOO based on the SVD.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Development of a shortened sniper rifle based on the SVD for Soviet airborne troops began at TsKIB SOO in 1977. In 1979, demonstration models of the SVU rifle designed by L. V. Bondarev were offered to parts of the VDV (Airborne Forces), but production did not proceed at that time. In 1991, serial production of the SVU began, with the first rifles entering service with the 106th Guards Airborne Division (where they underwent military trials). In the early 1990s, the rifle was proposed to the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Russia as a sniper weapon for urban conditions. The Ministry of Internal Affairs adopted the SVU, and also requested a variant capable of full-auto fire. In 1991, Bondarev created the automatic rifle designated OЦ-03AS - a shortened, automatic sniper rifle with bipods. Based on the operational experience of the SVU rifles during the war in Chechnya, the SVU-AS rifle was developed in 2001.
 
The accuracy of the SVU at short and medium ranges is approximately comparable to the SVD. The ability to fire in full-auto mode in the SVU-A and SVU-AS is intended only for emergency situations (e.g., in close combat posing a real threat to the sniper's life), as the use of a powerful cartridge and the light weight of the rifle lead to significant recoil during full-auto fire, and the small magazine does not allow for prolonged shooting. The SVU-A (OЦ-03A) is a variant with full-auto capability.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1990-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Raging Bull%c[0,140,140,140] - a revolver created by the Brazilian firearms manufacturer Taurus International. This weapon is positioned as a hunting revolver due to its caliber. For example, the .454 Casull allows hunting large animals such as the African buffalo or African elephant.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
From a technical standpoint, the Raging Bull is a revolver with a solid frame, a swing-out cylinder for reloading, and a double-action trigger mechanism (there are also modifications with a single-action trigger mechanism). The sighting devices are open - a fixed front sight and an adjustable rear sight. Alternative sighting devices can also be installed thanks to the optionally included Picatinny rail. To compensate for recoil when using powerful cartridges, a muzzle brake-compensator is introduced in the front part of the barrel, made in the form of slots on the sides of the front sight. The revolver frame can be made of steel, titanium, or stainless steel, and the grip is made of rubber. Depending on the cartridge used, the cylinder can hold 5, 6, 7, or 8 cartridges, and the barrel length can be 4, 5, 6, 8, or 10 inches.
 
The Raging Bull fits snugly in the hand and has a red rubber pad that reduces recoil, allowing the shooter to feel more comfortable.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]

%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Brazil
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: present

%c[0,153,255,255]TOZ-34%c[0,140,140,140] - a lightweight double-barreled hunting rifle with vertically aligned barrels, intended for commercial and amateur hunting.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The TOZ-34 was developed by gunsmith Nikolai Ivanovich Korovyakov. This rifle was intended for amateur hunters, commercial hunters, and sport shooters. Initially, the rifle was designed with a 12-gauge barrel, but the Tula Arms Plant planned to produce it in various versions - with smoothbore barrels ranging from 12 to 32 gauges and in combination with rifled barrels of different calibers. Since 1964, the rifle was produced individually at the Tula Arms Plant. In 1965, the TOZ-34 rifle was awarded a gold medal at the Leipzig Fair for its design originality, as it significantly differed from known domestic and foreign rifles at that time. Overall, it became the lightest mass-produced Soviet double-barreled hunting rifle of the 12th gauge. Since 1969, the rifle has been produced in large series.
 
The TOZ-34 is a hammerless double-barreled hunting rifle with vertically aligned barrels. At various times, rifles of 12, 16, 20, 28, and 32 gauges were produced. The weight of the TOZ-34 rifle reached 3.1 kg. The rifles feature modern sleek shapes, good balance, and high shooting accuracy. The 12th and 28th gauge rifles are produced in standard, individual, and souvenir versions. A limited number of 20th and 32nd gauge rifles were made %c[0,153,255,255](to switch to double-shot mode, use the firing mode switch key)%c[0,140,140,140].
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (RF)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1964-1995

%c[0,153,255,255]USAS-12%c[0,140,140,140] - an automatic shotgun developed by John Trevor in the late 1980s based on the ideas of another American designer, Maxwell Atchisson, who created the Atchisson Assault Shotgun.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Trevor worked at the small company Gilbert Equipment Co, which did not have production facilities. After a long search for potential manufacturers of the new weapon, a division of the South Korean conglomerate Daewoo - Daewoo Precision Industries - was chosen. Korean engineers made some modifications to the shotgun design, after which the USAS-12 went into production. Among the main buyers were police and military units in Southeast Asian countries. Gilbert Equipment Co tried to import semi-automatic versions for sale on the civilian market in the USA, but this was prohibited by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives. Variants assembled from South Korean parts were sold to US government and police units, assembled by RAMO Defense. There is no current information on the use of the USAS-12 by any organizations.
 
The USAS-12 uses a gas-operated mechanism with a barrel-locking system by a movable cylindrical part that rises up into a round hole in the barrel extension, similar to the AAS/AA-12. The hollow plastic stock is designed to absorb the recoil of the bolt carrier during firing, reducing the rate of fire and recoil. To ensure ambidexterity, the extractor window and charging handle cutouts are present on both sides of the barrel extension. The three-position safety-fire mode selector is similar to that of the M16 rifle. A carrying handle is integrated into the barrel extension.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: automatic shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA/South Korea
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1990

%c[0,153,255,255]Heckler & Koch USP%c[0,140,140,140] - a semi-automatic pistol developed by the German company Heckler & Koch. First introduced in 1993. Designed for police and military use.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Work on creating a new advanced pistol, primarily intended for the American market (both civilian and police), began at Heckler & Koch in mid-1989. The goal was to develop a versatile pistol that would have various trigger mechanisms (USM) options and meet the requirements of different customers, while also possessing high performance characteristics. The name of the new firearm - USP, stood for Universal Self-loading Pistol, meaning a universal semi-automatic pistol. The main requirements for the new pistol were ease of everyday use and reliability. In 1996, it was adopted by the U.S. Navy SEALs under the fleet index Mk 23 Mod 0. This pistol is a modification of the USP for the .45 ACP caliber, significantly heavier and more durable. It is considered the most accurate .45 caliber semi-automatic pistol to this day.
 
Currently, the HK USP is produced in the following calibers: .40 S&W, 9x19mm Parabellum, and .45 ACP. It is known for its high durability, reliability, and good shooting accuracy. Among its drawbacks are its significant size, even in the compact version, a relatively high center of gravity, and a massive slide, which somewhat complicates concealed carry. One of the distinctive features of the USP is its wide variety of modifications. There are currently nine different variants, although the system for these variants is generally the same.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1993-present

%c[0,153,255,255]FN FNX%c[0,140,140,140] - a semi-automatic pistol of joint Belgian-American production, giving its name to the pistol series of the same name.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Manufactured in Columbia (South Carolina, USA) at the FN America (FN USA) factories - the American division of the Belgian firearms company FN Herstal. The firearms are marked on the slide with "Fredericksburg, VA", corresponding to the location where the license for the production of this weapon was issued. It is produced in four modifications for the calibers 9x19mm Parabellum, .40 S&W, and .45 ACP. All modifications of this pistol share common features such as ambidextrous safety levers, ambidextrous magazine releases, and slide stops. All models are also equipped with a 3-dot sight with white or tritium inserts, a Picatinny rail, and a magazine load indicator on the right side. New models are made of tough plastic and come with three magazines and two (since 2012, four) interchangeable grip panels. The grips differ in thickness, design, and colors. Unlike pistols from Glock, SIG Sauer, Heckler & Koch, and many others, the FNX pistol is not widely spread in the market. It is used by some law enforcement agencies in the USA. Accessories and spare parts for it are produced by only a few companies. Some of the modifications include different barrel lengths, various triggers, safeties, and holsters.
 
By early October 2012, only the FNX-9 and FNX-40 variants had been developed, distinguished by their slide (matte black, blued, or stainless steel). In early October, the FNX-45 variant was developed, made of stainless steel with different finishes. It comes with magazines with 12 holes (marked from 4 to 15), which allows the shooter to more accurately determine the number of rounds in the magazine.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA/Belgium
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2012-present

%c[0,153,255,255]AS VAL Special Assault Rifle%c[0,140,140,140] - a silent automatic rifle developed by the TsNIITochMash in the town of Klimovsk by designers P. Serdyukov and V. Krasnikov in the late 1980s based on the silent sniper rifle "VSS". "Val" and "VSS" are visually similar and are 70% unified.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Until the 1970s, special forces units of the USSR mainly used modified samples of general-purpose small arms equipped with silencers and special subsonic bullet cartridges. Examples include the "Tishina" complexes based on the AKM and the "Kanareyka" based on the AKS-74U, as well as the PB and APB pistols. However, such solutions had their drawbacks (for example, significant increase in size with silencers on pistols, sharp decrease in effective firing range with the PBS-1 on rifles and its limited resource), so parallel to this, special-purpose narrow-focused samples were created at TsNIITochMash under the leadership of the Scientific Research Institute of the KGB and the GRU of the General Staff of the USSR, which could provide greater operational stealth for special units.
 
The AS VAL Special Assault Rifle is based on gas-operated automatics with a long stroke of the gas piston. It uses special 9mm subsonic cartridges SP-5 and SP-6. The sniper bullet of the SP-5 cartridge (with a steel core) penetrates Class 2 body armor or a 6mm steel sheet at a range of 100 meters, and a 2mm steel sheet at any sighting distance, while retaining enough energy to defeat a covered enemy. The armor-piercing SP-6 cartridge with an enlarged core made of hardened steel can disable a vehicle and even lightly armored vehicles. It penetrates an 8mm steel sheet at a range of 100 meters, and a 5mm steel sheet or Class 3 body armor at any sighting distance. These performance characteristics are comparable to those of standard 5.56mm and 5.45mm cartridges, which have several times greater muzzle energy. The rifle's magazines are interchangeable with those of the VSS Vintorez.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Assault Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1987-present

%c[0,153,255,255]SR-3 "Vikhr"%c[0,140,140,140] - a compact Russian assault rifle developed based on the silent assault rifle AS "Val".
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Development of a compact 9mm assault rifle based on the AS "Val" design began in 1989 by the designer A.D. Borisov at TsNIITochMash. The first prototype of the weapon (designated RG051) was manufactured in 1991. In 1994, with the participation of designer V.N. Levchenko, the second prototype of the rifle - SR-3 "Vikhr" - was created, which successfully completed state trials by the end of 1994. The final adjustments were made by designer A.I. Tashlykov. In 1995, SR-3 "Vikhr" rifles were put into experimental operation in special units, and by mid-1996, they were officially accepted into service. Later, an upgraded version, SR-3M, was developed, which was intended to combine the advantages of the SR-3, VSS, and AS. The SR-3 is unified with the AS "Val" in its main components, which has a positive effect on the production and operation of the weapon. The SR-3's mechanism is similar to the AS "Val": it uses gas-operated automatics and locks the barrel with a 6-point bolt. The SR-3 can be equipped with a suppressor.
 
When using special armor-piercing SP-6 cartridges, the rifle theoretically allows successful engagement of targets wearing Class 5 body armor (ceramic plate) at distances up to 25 meters, and Class 2 body armor (steel) at distances up to 100 meters. The compact size of the SR-3 facilitates concealed carry. The SR-3M differs by its ability to use a suppressor, metal 30-round magazines, installation of optical and night sights, a left-folding skeleton stock (borrowed from the AS "Val"), a lever-type safety similar to the SVD, and a new stock with a folding additional grip.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
