%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1996-present

%c[0,153,255,255]KRISS Vector%c[0,140,140,140] - submachine gun developed by Transformational Defense Industries. The KRISS Vector uses a semi-free bolt system that directs the recoil-operated bolt group downwards at a steep angle. This system is called Kriss Super V.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The main goal of the designers of this weapon was to achieve the maximum possible accuracy in burst fire using .45 ACP cartridges, which have high stopping power, while minimizing the size and overall weight of the submachine gun. The engineers of the manufacturer Transformational Defense Industries, Inc. (TDI) decided to use the patents of French designer Renaud Kerbrat. Prototype testing of this weapon began in 2005. The test results showed that when firing from the TDI KRISS Super V submachine gun in burst mode, weapon recoil is minimal, which ensures high accuracy of automatic fire and excellent weapon control during shooting. By these parameters, the TDI KRISS Super V submachine gun significantly surpasses such models as the German 11.43mm submachine gun HK UMP45.
 
Currently, there are three main variants of the submachine gun: the "combat" variant Vector SMG, intended for law enforcement agencies, law enforcement, and military with a short (140 mm) barrel and automatic firing modes; the civilian carbine Vector CRB/SO, with an extended barrel up to 406 mm (according to US legislation, civilian long-barreled weapons must have a barrel length of at least 16 inches, otherwise it is considered short-barreled, obtaining a license for which is much more difficult in several states), over which a sleeve simulating a suppressor is worn for greater aesthetics, and with the ability to fire only in semi-automatic mode; and the Vector SDP with a 5.5-inch (140mm) barrel and semi-automatic trigger mechanism, considered a pistol under US law. All variants of the Kriss Vector have special Picatinny rails, mounted on top of the barrel box and under the barrel, allowing the attachment of various types of mechanical, collimator, and optical sights, flashlights, laser target designators, and additional front grips.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2009-present

%c[0,153,255,255]VSS Special Sniper Rifle Vintorez%c[0,140,140,140] - silent sniper rifle for special purpose units. GRAU index - 6P29. Developed in the 1980s at TsNIITochMash in Klimovsk under the leadership of P. I. Sedyukov and with the participation of V. F. Krasnikov. The name "Vintorez" remained in common use after its use in design documentation. It should not be confused with the AS "Val", which was created based on the VSS and visually resembles the VSS.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The new rifle passed preliminary tests; however, in 1985, requirements for a silent automatic weapon were approved, based on which it had to ensure the defeat of an enemy in 6B2 body armor at a distance of 400 m. Due to this, the rifle's designers concluded that the RG037 cartridge would be ineffective against enemies wearing future individual protection means, so further work on it was discontinued, and the sniper rifle was completely redesigned for the soon-to-be-developed 9x39 mm cartridge. It was adopted by the special units of the KGB and GRU in 1987 and later served as the basis for the development of the AS "Val". The VSS has a "swallowtail" mounting bracket, allowing it to mount scopes like the PSO-1, PSV (1P43), any standard night sights (MBNP-1, NSPUM, NSPU-3), as well as 4x34 PO scopes with a special adapter and the combined optic sight KOP-2; an open sector sight is also installed on the suppressor casing.
 
The VSS officially has a resource of 1500 shots, but with timely maintenance, cleaning, and lubrication, this weapon can withstand up to 5000 shots without a decrease in combat quality. The VSS is not completely silent. The sound of a shot is approximately equivalent to a small-caliber rifle and can only be distinguished in silence, which is significantly better than with weapons equipped with suppressors. However, a weapon equipped with a suppressor and using a subsonic cartridge, like the VSS, can often have a comparable shot volume.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1987-present

%c[0,153,255,255]VSSM%c[0,140,140,140] - special modernized sniper rifle VSSM (6P29M).
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Regarding the "Ratnik" theme at TsNIITochMash (Klimovsk), a modernized 9mm sniper rifle 6P29M is being developed - an advancement of the silent sniper complex (SSC) VSS ("special sniper rifle") "Vintorez", adopted in 1987. Now, Vintorez can be equipped with bipods, a variable magnification scope, and a new magazine holding 30 rounds. The stock has been changed, replacing the wooden stock with a polymer one that is impact-resistant; the exact composition is unknown. The stock is rubberized, the grip has serrations, and it is now lighter with added adjustments for individual shooters. Another new change is the barrel cover, which is no longer removable but hinged and foldable, detachable only during complete disassembly. Additionally, a Picatinny rail has been added to the cover, removing the side dovetail, which is now unnecessary, allowing any optic to be mounted on the barrel cover. A new and useful addition is the Picatinny rail attachments on the barrel with rails on three sides. It is now possible to install a large number of additional devices on the weapon, which was problematic before. It can accommodate an additional grip, tactical flashlight, and bipods.
 
This new weapon will be in high demand among special forces. In addition to ergonomic changes, the modernized models have a new resource guarantee for shooters, from five to ten thousand rounds, doubled. This was made possible by new materials used in both internal components and the body. The new standard 1P86 scope for these models is an optical scope with variable magnification of 1 to 4.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: Present

%c[0,153,255,255]PP-19-01 Vityaz%c[0,140,140,140] - Russian submachine gun, developed in 2004 by the Izhevsk Mechanical Plant (Izhmash). Depending on the version, it is based on the AKS-74U or the AK-105.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The weapon was developed by the Izhevsk Mechanical Plant (Izhmash) for the Russian Internal Troops special forces unit "Vityaz", after which it was named. It is used to engage live forces (including those protected by body armor) and non-armored vehicles (both cars and trucks). The "Vityaz" is produced in two variants: the base PP-19-01 mod.10 and the modified PP-19-01 mod.20 "Vityaz-SN". The second variant differs by moving the safety and bolt handle to the left side, and adding an additional Picatinny rail on the barrel cover. One of the main differences between the "Vityaz-SN" and the base PP-19-01 is that it is based not on the AKS-74U, but on the AK-105. This is primarily expressed in the replacement of the barrel cover and fire control group with those of the AK-100 series, the installation of standard handguards and gas tube covers from the AK-74M series, and the replacement of the folding sight of the AKS-74U with an open sector sight.
 
A standard submachine gun used by personnel of the Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs (MVD), Russian police, National Guard (Rosgvardia), Federal Penitentiary Service (FSIN), and other law enforcement agencies. It represents a further development of the PP-19 "Bizon".
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Submachine Gun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2008-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Vepr%c[0,140,140,140] - family of self-loading hunting carbines developed based on the 7.62mm Kalashnikov assault rifle. The ancestor of the family was the SOK-94 carbine, designed in 1995 by the designers of the Vyatsko-Polyansky Machine-Building Plant "Molot" P.M. Mokrushin, V.I. Lyamin, A.I. Kabatchikov, and others.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Vepr carbines are produced in calibers 5.45x39mm, 7.62x39mm, 7.62x54mm R, 9x9mm, 9.6/53 Lancaster, .366 TKM, .223 Remington, .243 Winchester, .308 Winchester, .30-06 Springfield in multiple modifications and variants. The carbines are characterized by high reliability and fault tolerance in various operating conditions. The carbine's automatic reload is accomplished by utilizing the energy of powder gases vented from the barrel into the gas chamber during firing, and the energy of the return spring. To increase barrel life, the barrel channel and chamber are chrome-plated, and to reduce flash during firing, some models are equipped with a flash suppressor or compensator at the muzzle. To prevent possible inertial firing pin impact, the bolt striker is spring-loaded. For mounting an optical sight, a side mount of the "dovetail" type on the barrel cover or a Picatinny rail on the barrel cover is used. The sighting rail of the Vepr series carbines allows effective shooting at distances up to 300m.
 
In the civilian firearms markets of Italy, Turkey, Germany, and some countries in the northwest part of North America, Vepr carbines have been popular among fans of the Kalashnikov assault rifle due to their structural similarity and the use, in some models, of the native 7.62x39mm cartridge for this rifle. The carbines are intended for commercial and amateur hunting of medium and large game. The "Vepr-Hunter" is produced in two versions: the VPO-101 "Vepr-Hunter" for the 7.62x51mm cartridge; the VPO-102 "Vepr-Hunter M" for the 7.62x63mm cartridge.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Self-loading carbine
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1995-present

%c[0,153,255,255]WA2000%c[0,140,140,140] (Walther Automat 2000) - a bullpup configuration sniper rifle developed by the Walther arms consortium.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The development of the rifle began after the tragedy at the 1972 Munich Olympics, which showed the unpreparedness of the police and special services of West Germany for such attacks. Alongside the complete modernization of the security forces, the Walther arms consortium was tasked with developing an effective urban and high-precision weapon. The rifle was produced from 1982 until November 1988. Production was discontinued due to the excessive cost of the product (Walther offered this sniper complex for 9885 German marks) compared to other sniper rifles (for comparison, in 1982 the Steyr SSG-69 sniper rifle was offered for $1974 USD). The last units were sold in 1988 for prices ranging from 9 to 12.5 thousand USD. Currently, it is a collector's rarity due to the small number of units produced. According to some estimates, the current price of a WA2000 rifle fluctuates around $40-50 thousand, and according to some sources, up to $75 thousand. According to Walther, only 176 rifles of this type were produced. The remaining units were produced by third-party gunsmiths due to the rifle's popularity among collectors. Specialists note the excellent ergonomics of the rifle, characteristic of many German, Austrian, and Swiss designs. Its application is possible only as a "police" rifle due to its extremely low resistance to pollution and also had a number of shortcomings. Initially, the rifle was developed for four types of cartridges: 7.62x67 mm (.300 Winchester Magnum), 7.62x51 mm NATO, 7.5x54 mm French rifle cartridge, and 7.5x55 mm Swiss rifle cartridge. The main cartridge was supposed to be .300 Winchester Magnum (.300 Win. Mag.), created based on the large-caliber .338 Winchester Magnum cartridge (providing high accuracy at medium distances and used by police snipers), but it did not gain wide distribution among military and police forces. Currently used as hunting (for which it was developed).
 
The rifle is made in a bullpup configuration, which allowed maintaining the barrel length typical of sniper rifles but reducing the overall length of the weapon. The WA-2000 is a self-loading rifle, the action of which is due to the diversion of powder gases from the barrel channel. The rifle does not have an open sight. A mounting for installing optical and night sights is provided on the barrel cover. The standard optical sight installed was the SchmidtBender 2.5-10x60. The bipods have a non-standard design; their swivel unit can move along a special rail located above the barrel. The rifle seems to hang under the bipod mounting assembly, which positively affects the convenience of aiming. The wooden stock is adjustable in length and cheek rest position for the shooter. The rifle had a modular design, which provided quick barrel and bolt changes using a connecting sleeve located above the pistol grip. Due to the particular design feature, it is impossible to shoot from the left shoulder with the rifle, as the ejection port is very close to the shooter. There is no provision for changing the side of ejection of spent casings to the left side on the rifle.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Sniper Rifle
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1982-1988

%c[0,153,255,255]Walther P99%c[0,140,140,140] - a German pistol developed by the Walther firearms company since the early 1990s. The project, named Hammerless Pistol - HLP (hammerless pistol), was led by Horst Wesp. Serial production of the pistol began in 1999.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
Development of the P99 pistol by Carl Walther Sportwaffen GmbH began in 1994. The main task was to create a modern pistol for police and self-defense that could achieve greater commercial success than its predecessor, the Walther P88, which, despite its excellent characteristics, turned out to be too expensive. The development of the P99 was completed by 1996, and the result was quite successful. It has excellent ergonomics, is reliable, and is produced in several variants with different trigger systems. Since 1999, all variants of the P99 series have also been produced in .40SW caliber. In 2004, the Walther P99 family underwent some changes - the shape of the trigger guard and the arrangement of serrations on the slide were slightly changed, and the models were re-marked; for example, the P990 variant was renamed to P99 DAO. In addition, compact versions with shortened barrels and grips were added to the model range.
 
The frame of the pistol is made of polymer materials and comes in black or dark green ("military" variant). A distinctive feature of the P99 is the removable rear part of the grip, and the pistol comes with 3 interchangeable rear parts of different sizes for fitting to each shooter's hand. Guide rails for mounting a laser pointer or flashlight are integrated into the front part of the frame under the barrel. The pistol can also be equipped, upon request from relevant organizations, with a special extended barrel with threading at the muzzle, allowing the installation of a proprietary silencer.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pistol
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Germany
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1999-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Mossberg 500%c[0,140,140,140] - a family of multi-shot shotguns from the USA.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Mossberg 500 shotgun appeared in late 1961. The Mossberg 500 is a typical pump-action shotgun, where the action is operated by moving the fore-end back and forth, and the chamber is locked by a moving bolt inside the receiver, which moves up and down in the cutout of the barrel extension. Cartridges are fed from an under-barrel tubular magazine, which can vary in length and capacity (from 4 to 8 rounds). On top of the barrel, there are four threaded holes designed for mounting rails for optical, collimator, or diopter sights. Most components of the design are known for their high strength and reliability, which combined with a reasonable cost has made the Mossberg 500 very popular in law enforcement. Mossberg also produces combat variants. A characteristic feature of this pump-action shotgun is the safety, located on top of the barrel and operated by the thumb. According to most shooters, such a safety, similar to the safeties of double-barrel shotguns, is more convenient than the safety button on the trigger guard, which is controlled by the index finger and is used on most pump-action shotguns.
 
Currently, the Mossberg 500 is produced in various modifications, intended for hunting, law enforcement, security, and self-defense. The Mossberg 590, a model developed in the 1970s as a replacement for the Mossberg 500, differs in the attachment of the barrel to the magazine (nut instead of a screw), stronger bluing, and several other changes. It is also worth mentioning that during the Vietnam War, Mossberg 500 shotguns were loaded not with shot or bullets, but with solid metal arrow-shaped projectiles. In the jungle conditions, these projectiles did not spread like shot but hit the target despite significant obstacles such as leaves and even small branches.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pump-action shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1961-present

%c[0,153,255,255]Remington 870%c[0,140,140,140] - an American shotgun, first introduced in 1950 by Remington Arms. The Remington 870 is one of the most famous pump-action shotguns in the world due to its distinctive action.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The Remington 870 is the fourth popular model of pump-action shotguns developed by Remington. John Pedersen developed the delicate Remington Model 10 (later improved as the Remington Model 29). John Browning developed the Remington Model 17 (which was borrowed by Ithaca as the Ithaca 37), which became the basis for the Remington Model 31. While the Model 31 gained popularity, it was overshadowed by the Winchester Model 12 in the market competition. Remington aimed to correct this situation and in 194 introduced the modern, improved, durable, reliable, and relatively affordable Remington Model 870 shotgun. The Model 870 had steady sales, reaching 2 million units by 1973 (ten times more than the Model 31). With the introduction of the "Express" model - a cheaper alternative to the original Wingmaster, sales reached 7 million units. On April 13, 2009, the 10-millionth Remington 870 shotgun was sold, setting a new sales record. The Remington 870 is a shotgun and a shotgun with an under-barrel tubular magazine and a longitudinal sliding fore-end ("pump-action" shotgun). It locks with a rocking bolt located in the receiver behind the barrel extension. The barrel is made of steel. Under the barrel, there is a tubular magazine for 8 cartridges. In the hunting version, it holds 3-4 cartridges.
 
The Remington 870 has been produced in various modifications, suitable for military and police use, as well as for hunters and sportsmen, and also for self-defense. Police modifications usually have a barrel length of 355 or 457 millimeters with a "cylinder" or "improved cylinder" bore, allowing for both shot and slugs, as well as various special ammunition types such as gas grenades or rubber trauma bullets. In 1966, the shotgun was adopted by the US Marine Corps, in 1967 - by certain categories of US Air Force personnel, and subsequently purchased by the FBI. In 1987, it was recommended for police use and remains in service with the US Armed Forces, used by several police departments and private security firms. In 2001, the replacement of Remington 870 shotguns in service with US military police and Marine Corps units began with the M1014 shotgun.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: Pump-action shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USA
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1951-present

%c[0,153,255,255]MTs-255%c[0,140,140,140] is a Russian revolver shotgun fed by a 5-round swing-out cylinder. It is produced by the TsKIB SOO, Central Design and Research Bureau of Sporting and Hunting Arms.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
There is no safety catch. The shotgun can be fired with the trigger cocked or self-cocking. The barrel is tilted to the left side when reloading or loading the shotgun. To prevent powder gases from escaping into the gap between the drum and the barrel, special chambers on the sides of the barrel are used, and the drum is fed forward when the trigger is pulled. The trigger mechanism is made in one piece, which greatly simplifies cleaning when disassembling the gun. The MTs-255 shotgun is one of the most original civilian weapon designs, developed by Russian gunsmiths at the end of the last century. The unusual idea is the use of a revolver scheme to create a shotgun smoothbore.
 
The revolver shotgun did not last very long on the assembly line: from 2000 to 2007. There were two reasons why the CKIB SOO management decided to close the project. First, the shotgun did not justify itself from a commercial point of view. Strong competition in the market and the impossibility to reduce the price led to the fact that hunters did not show proper interest in the revolver design. Secondly, in order to prevent bankruptcy, the company merged with a «related» organization - Tula KBP. After that, it went headlong into defense orders.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: revolver shotgun
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2000-2007

%c[0,153,255,255]RSh-12%c[0,140,140,140] - 12.7mm assault revolver. It is considered and recognised as the most large-calibre personal close combat weapon. It was developed and designed in 2000 in Tula, at the Central Committee of the Institute of Sporting and Hunting Weapons of the Russian Federation.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The development and production of revolvers is the last thing that the domestic arms industry is associated with. Nevertheless, such weapons are also created in Russia. The most striking and most representative example in this regard is the RSh-12, a revolver that was created directly for the special forces.
 
The RSh-12 revolver has a solid metal frame. The revolver is powered by a five-round drum, which is hinged to the left. The ammunition is always drawn from the lower chamber. Despite the solid calibre, the recoil shoulder of the Russian revolver is significantly lower than that of its counterparts from other countries. An interesting detail is the reinforced barrel cover with ventilation. There is a Picatinny rail above the barrel for mounting tactical accessories.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: revolver
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: Russia
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 2000-present

%c[0,153,255,255]RGN%c[0,140,140,140] (Ruchnaya Granata Nastupatel'naya, "Hand Grenade Offensive") is an offensive Soviet blast hand grenade introduced during the Soviet-Afghan war alongside the RGO to replace the earlier F-1, RG-42, and RGD-5 hand grenades which proved to be inadequate in the mountainous terrain of Afghanistan.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The grenade is designed to hit living forces in offensive combat. The grenade's fragmentation radius is 8 meters, and the radius of possible defeat is 24 meters. The grenade is painted in olive grayish color.
 
It consists of a smooth spherical aluminium body, internally scored to generate fragments upon detonation. Externally, the RGN is similar to the RGO hand grenade and shares the same UDZS fuze. The UDZS fuze has both impact and time delay functions, the impact fuze arms after a pyrotechnic delay of 1 to 1.8 seconds. If the grenade strikes an object after this time a spherical lead shot filled impact weight will trigger detonation. If the grenade has not struck anything after 3.5 to 4 seconds the second pyrotechnic delay will detonate the grenade, allowing the grenade to cause an airburst over a target area if necessary. The impact fuze detonates when it hits any kind of terrain, including sand, snow, or water.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: hand grenade
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1981-present

%c[0,153,255,255]RGO%c[0,140,140,140] (Ruchnaya Granata Oboronitel'naya, ("Hand Grenade Defensive")) is a defensive Soviet fragmentation hand grenade introduced mid 1980s alongside the RGN during the Soviet-Afghan War to replace the earlier F-1, RG-42, and RGD-5 hand grenades, which proved to be inadequate in the mountains of Afghanistan.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Information:%c[0,140,140,140] 
The grenade is designed to defeat manpower in defensive combat. The grenade is delivered to the target only by throwing it by the soldier's hand. The grenade's fragmentation radius is 50 meters, the radius of possible defeat is 100 meters. The grenade is similar to the F-1 grenade, but differs from it by the use of a combined percussion and remote fuse. The combat grenade is painted in olive grayish color.
 
It consists of a double layered steel pre-fragmented body. Unlike the two-part hemispherical RGN, the RGO is made up of 4 quadrants: the upper hemispheres are internally segmented to provide fragmentation and are covered by a smooth exterior, while the lower hemispheres are segmented in a diamond-pattern cross-hatching in the outer shell that provides additional fragmentation. It uses the UDZS dual action fuze (also used on the RGN), which has both impact and time delay functions. The impact fuze arms after a pyrotechnic delay of 1 to 1.8 seconds. If the impact fuze has not triggered the grenade after 3.2 to 4.2 seconds a second pyrotechnic delay triggers the grenade, allowing this grenade to be thrown to produce an airburst over a target area, if necessary. The RGO is designed to detonate when it hits any kind of terrain including sand, snow, or water.
 
%c[0,0,255,30]Additional:%c[0,140,140,140]
 
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Type: hand grenade
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Country of Origin: USSR (Russia)
%c[0,200,0,255]• %c[0,140,140,140]Years of production: 1981-present
